and translate the less praiseworthy and commendable intentions inherent in their genesis.
And much needs to change regarding the lack of independence of the Judicial Power, which is subject to excessive influence not only from political power but also from economic actors.
Without a free and independent justice system, there is no rule of law, human rights are not guaranteed, there is no foreign investment, and there is no progress.
But the blame for this disillusionment also lies heavily within this Parliament and, fundamentally, with many Member States.
I would like you, for a few seconds, to imagine yourself as a citizen of a European country that, due to the twists of history, is still not a member of the European Union.
what we would expect from the Union.
Solidarity.

Yet many chancelleries, solely concerned with not offending or displeasing Moscow, exploited and abused the ongoing internal conflicts in Kyiv to avoid unequivocally stating that Ukraine is an independent and sovereign country.
Thus, if that is the freely expressed will of the majority of its people and provided they meet the established criteria, Ukraine may aspire to become, in the future, a member of the European Union.
I would like to emphasize that in island regions, air transport is sometimes the only means of access, and operating conditions are constrained by the scale of their passenger and cargo flows.

While there are regions in Soares where the abundance of these flows is attractive to many airlines, drawing competition and low transportation costs and thereby improving accessibility, other regions, such as the Azores, have these flows still limited, making them unattractive to airline operators.

As a result, airfares are higher, representing a barrier to population mobility and a constraint on attracting tourists capable of enabling this vast potential for economic development and diversification to flourish and become consolidated.
This situation is particularly detrimental during periods of crisis such as the one we are experiencing.

That is why temporary support is essential to stimulate increased flows, enabling the market to function properly, while also integrating these regions with high tourism potential into the objectives of the trans-European networks in connecting Europe's peripheries.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I rise today in this Chamber with a voice choked by the distress of someone who has personally experienced and witnessed the tragedy that struck Madeira last Saturday.

My words are, of course, intended to associate myself with the feelings of pain and mourning that have affected the families of the deceased victims, whose number currently stands at 42, to whom I extend my tribute and respect for their losses.

The true extent of this disaster remains to be revealed, as search and rescue teams, who have been working tirelessly since Saturday in a Herculean effort that I commend, are only now beginning to reach the most isolated communities.
and there is concern that the number of victims may increase.

The scenario depicts widespread destruction, with significant material damage to access routes, roads, and complete devastation of key locations, as well as critical services such as water supply and electricity.
Approximately 600 people have been displaced, having lost their homes and personal belongings.
The economic and social impact of this catastrophe prevents people from resuming a normal life course.
But it is now necessary to look ahead.
I also need to convey a message of solidarity, hope, and trust to people.
Help is needed. It is in this chapter that the European Union and, in particular, the Parliament have a role to play. They are essential in mobilizing the Solidarity Fund.
And when we are called upon to intervene, we must do so swiftly, for we cannot ask people to wait, especially when they need us most.

I appeal here to the Commission and its President, Dr. Barroso, particularly to the Commissioner for Regional Development, Johan Assane, and I welcome your initiative to visit Madeira at the earliest opportunity.
Requesting that he also be the bearer of this message of hope, assistance, and support for reconstruction. Because now is the time to rebuild what nature, unfortunately once again, has taken from us.
But I am certain we will do it. Because, as the song of Madeira taught me, the Madeiran people are humble, stoic, and brave.
It is the people who, between the hard rock, cultivated the land. They are heroes of labor in the mountainous backlands.
Through wood he will honor his history, and along the path of labor he will strive and achieve his well-being and glory.
We begin this plenary session today with a minute of silence.

And it was a minute of silence with two meanings: one of solidarity with the victims of this disaster that struck Madeira and their families, and another of tribute to all workers who have tirelessly assisted these victims.
But the moment of silence should make us reflect, and should also guide us toward a reflection that goes beyond the one we are here engaged in.
What we observed in Madeira is a phenomenon that will tend to recur increasingly.
Climate change causes extreme events to recur, and we must have responses ready for them.
We cannot prevent natural disasters, but we should not be condemned to their always having such dramatic consequences for people's lives.
That is why we must advocate for planning and zoning policies that are oriented toward the public good.

But I wish to emphasize, Mr. President, that what is most important and what has brought us here is that the European Commission, the European Parliament, and the European institutions must have the capacity to provide an urgent response to an emergency situation. For this, the necessary financial resources and the required speed are essential.
A gesture of solidarity in memory of the families and friends of the victims of the tragic events that occurred on Saturday on the island of Madeira.

As an Azorean, as a Portuguese citizen, and as a European, I cannot but appeal to Parliament and the Union to demonstrate active solidarity in support of the Autonomous Region of Madeira and its people.
It is in regions where the presence of the Union needs to be felt most, and precisely at these moments when its solidarity is most needed that it is also most important for it to be felt.

I too wish to express my solidarity with the people of Madeira and to urgently appeal to the European institutions, and in particular to the Commission, to unlock all available resources, all means at their disposal, in order to provide this assistance.
I would also like to emphasize that when disasters of this nature occur, it is much more difficult to address their consequences when they happen in poor countries and poor regions.

However, in the case of wood, we must also consider that the economic foundation of the wood industry is directly affected by the fact that, depending on tourism, it has a dual consequence: when the area's natural beauty, accessibility, and quality of life are completely destroyed.

Therefore, there is indeed a different situation when we face problems of this severity in poor countries and in poor regions, and particularly in mountainous and tourist areas, as is the case of Madeira.
Hence, it is absolutely essential that all the adaptations that were planned, requested, and recalled here in relation to the Solidarity Fund not be delayed any further.

that they immediately come into effect, as due to climate change and these processes, unfortunately, such events will recur, particularly in Europe’s poorest regions, which experience torrential rainfall and extreme droughts during the summer. Thank you once again, Mr. President.
Commissioner and Council, we are here discussing the reinvention of the energy system at the European scale through investment in low-carbon technologies.
I believe it is one of the most important challenges, as indeed referred to in the communication provided.
and it is one of the challenges we must address centrally. But there is no clarification here, which is why it is important to raise these questions and request clarification.
It appears that, besides the lack of clarification regarding funding, there is also a lack of political clarity.
Discussions focus on energy efficiency, carbon capture, nuclear power, biofuels, solar energy, and even consider energy efficiency as the most cost-effective means of reducing emissions.

It surprises me that there are other, much cheaper ways to reduce carbon emissions that are not even mentioned in this communication, such as, for example, immediately reducing greenhouse gas consumption.
microgeneration, distribution, and different forms of distribution and diverse projects that do not need to go through large structures and access to energy.
I believe that would certainly help clarify the financial aspects, making the plan cheaper.

And therefore, within this political clarification and within this financial clarification, there remains the need to address what appears to me to be an absolutely crucial point: the emphasis that is constantly placed on public-private partnerships as if they were the solution to everything.
When asked where the money comes from, they tell us from the Commission that it comes from where it is.
I confess that I am not any clearer when told the money will come from where it is, without being told exactly where that is.

And therefore, this emphasis on public-private partnerships—systematically advocated when discussing low-carbon technologies—leaves us with a clear certainty: knowing neither the source of the funding nor whether the partnership is public or private, we already know from the outset who will bear the cost.
It is the taxpayers who pay initially, and it is the consumers—who happen to also be taxpayers—who end up paying in the end.

And we know that those who pay the least are the participants, the private institutions and organizations that carry out the investment, are compensated for it, but ultimately end up monopolizing the profits.
And without this clarification, it seems to me that once again we are systematically passing on to future generations the bill for the transition of the European energy model.

Thank you, Mr. President. The European Union recently concluded, within the framework of the World Trade Organization, an agreement with countries in Latin America, in which it committed to substantially and progressively reducing tariffs on imports of bananas from these countries.

I draw your attention to the fact that such an agreement has directly negative effects on European banana producers, particularly on banana producers from Madeira in Portugal, but also, and even more severely, on the producers from the Canary Islands in Spain.

I recall that the situation of producers in these regions is already seriously hindered by the physical and orographic characteristics of the islands and by the size of their holdings, which forces them to face persistent difficulties that hinder their development.

It is therefore urgent to take measures to protect the producers of bananas, timber, and canaries, given the serious and immediate impact that the new Geneva Agreement will have on the European Union's import market, and consequently on the production and marketing of bananas from these overseas regions.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Commissioner, as rapporteur for opinion of the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs, I would like to begin by

Congratulations the Member of Parliament who presented it, even in his forced absence, and highlight the way in which he skillfully prepared this important report, thereby facilitating the main objective.

This primary objective is, without further delay, to simplify procedures and accelerate the implementation of programs funded by the Cohesion Fund, the structural funds, and the Regional Development Fund.
In the context of the current financial, economic, and social crisis, pressure on national financial resources has been increasing.
This pressure will decrease with better utilization of community funding, enabling faster delivery of these resources to the beneficiaries most affected by the economic breakdown.
Over 20 million Europeans are unemployed, meaning 4 million more than a year ago. And unfortunately, the trend is set to rise.

This situation requires ensuring the proper implementation of cohesion programs, as they represent the most powerful and relevant lever of support for the real economy, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises and employment.
SMEs are the driving force behind the European economy and the key actors in sustainable growth and the creation of jobs in both quantity and quality.
The continuation of simplification and clarification of the rules governing cohesion policy will undoubtedly have a positive impact on the pace of program implementation, particularly because it will
national, regional, and local authorities, the possibility of resorting to clearer and less bureaucratic rules that will allow greater flexibility in adapting programs to new challenges. Many thanks.
Thank you, Mr. President. Of course, this crisis was an unforeseen event, and of course the precautionary principle had to take priority.
but there are lessons to be learned, and the first is that for five days no sufficiently strong and politically responsible European voice was heard.
And that voice was missing in terms of defending passengers' interests, clarifying their rights, seeking alternative transportation options, and coordinating solutions.

A second conclusion is that the way out of the crisis cannot appear, to the eyes of the average citizen, to be a struggle between those seeking to avoid losses and those striving to uphold the principle of precaution.
There must be clear transparency and clear objectivity regarding the conditions under which one exits a situation of total exclusion from the European space.
And therefore, the improvement of scientific testing and coordination also at the level...

Thank you, Mr. President. I would also like to begin by congratulating my colleague Tikal on her work and perseverance in this highly important file, and on the efforts she has made to improve the energy performance of buildings and to build a more sustainable Europe.
Energy expenditure in the European Union, as we know, is far above what is acceptable, which is why advancing proposals of this nature is so important.
It is high time we adopt an ambitious policy at the European level and complement this ambitious policy with additional measures, particularly given the crisis situation we are experiencing.
The construction sector is regarded as one of the markets with the greatest potential in terms of energy economy, but that is not all.
And it is not only that; for this reason, it is an area par excellence for the implementation of public policies. In this sense, Mr. President, I would like to conclude.
We therefore have the opportunity to invest in improving the energy efficiency of buildings and their potential to create millions of jobs in the coming years.
and not only in new buildings, but also in the rehabilitation and restoration of existing buildings that are degraded or inadequate.

Let us therefore use this legislation as a fundamental contribution to revitalizing the European economy, and we must not continue to fail to capitalize on investments that can and should be strategic for recovery from the crisis. Let us hope that the Commission provides investment and that Member States know how to make the most of it. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I would like to begin by congratulating my colleague Mandas on the work accomplished in this report, as well as thanking the colleagues who collaborated with him on this dossier.

In fact, the mere fact that this proposal simplifies and improves the existing regulatory framework—by consolidating into a single regulation all existing legislation, including three foundational directives concerning the designation and labelling of textile products—is in itself a significant achievement.

Moreover, this proposal will have a positive impact on private stakeholders and public authorities, and will also provide a framework for new fibers developed, thus encouraging innovation in the textile and clothing sector.
This review will also enhance the transparency of the process for adding new fibers to the list of harmonized fiber names.
There can be no doubt that the parties involved will gain advantages from this regulation.
Let us consider, the textile industry will benefit from cost savings in administrative expenses and the ability to market new fibers earlier.
On their part, the authorities of the Member States will not need to transpose the changes into national legislation, which will enable a significant cost saving for these Member States.
And consumers will have the assurance that the fibers so labeled meet specific characteristics, while also benefiting from the fact that the new fibers reach the market earlier.
For the European industry, the introduction of these new fibers is important as a means of promoting innovation, while also increasing the level of information provided to consumers.
In the current context of a globalized world, with emerging economies and increasingly expanded markets, in which millions of products produced by millions of companies circulate,
The bet on innovation and the speed with which this innovation reaches consumers is absolutely crucial for businesses, especially for small and medium-sized enterprises.
that the application of this regulation may enhance its competitiveness.
These small and medium-sized enterprises will undoubtedly benefit from this new regulation, gaining market share, and through this means will contribute to increasing employment in Europe.
Thank you very much, Madam President, Madam Minister, Commissioner. The shortage of organs for transplantation is a growing problem.

It has already been mentioned here several times, and it is a problem that poses challenges not only in terms of public health but also issues related to the strengthening of the black market and organ trafficking, neither of which enhances our healthcare systems nor our citizens.

And therefore, we must combat this crime, and combat it through legislation such as the proposals we are discussing here. In my view, this is not only more than important and more than relevant, but essential; and for this reason, I wish to focus on the most significant dimensions and issues raised by these two proposals.
Because the system we have so far, without these two proposals, is one of enormous inequalities.
inequalities among member states, inequalities among existing systems, inequalities in access to organs, inequalities among citizens, which I believe we simply cannot tolerate
Therefore, it is important to emphasize that these inequalities are decreasing. And these proposals aim precisely to combat this.
I believe that a European system that nonetheless maintains certain specificities of some countries, so they are not forced to adopt more restrictive systems, is the solution to this problem.
And the work carried out by the rapporteurs, Mr. Nicolás I and Mr. Perello Rodrigues, represents work of utmost importance and valuable contribution to the issue we are facing.
If we are discussing proposals based on and supported by issues such as gift-giving, volunteering, gratuitousness, confidentiality, and data protection,

Traceability, accountability, and here I believe it is important—on the Parliament's side—that this extends all the way to patient recovery, and not merely to donation, as well as cooperation among Member States. I believe we are on the right path.
I should only mention, however, in relation to donations between living persons, I welcome the inclusion and expansion, this expansion carried out with rights.

Obviously ensuring all rights and all conditions, but very importantly, going beyond the limited cycle of the conventional family, because otherwise it would merely be another form of achieving other forms of equality.

Therefore, promoting the DAD and the Main Transplant Fund, and safeguarding patient data and safety, seems to me more than sufficient reason for all of us to stand together tomorrow and vote in favor of these two proposals of utmost importance to humanity.

Mr. President, Minister, and Commissioner, the ability to replace deteriorated human organs with organs from another person, preventing rejection, is one of the great achievements of contemporary medicine.

European benchmarking reveals that medium-sized countries like Portugal perform very well in such comparisons, while larger countries like Spain are currently recognized as leaders in organizational practices and best practices.
some principles and practices to safeguard.
First, strengthening scientific knowledge and information that increasingly encourage living donation and generalize post-mortem donation. Second, the prohibition of the commercialization of organs.

not only within Member States but also beyond them, preventing transplant tourism and combating organ trafficking. Thirdly, the proactive promotion of donation in hospitals as a means of overcoming shortages.

Finally, Madam President, ensuring equal access to transplantation for all who need it and who meet clinically harmonized criteria, in accordance with the principle of universal access to health— a hallmark of the Social Europe model of which we are proud.
I congratulate the rapporteurs on the work accomplished.
I would like to emphasize that it is with great satisfaction that the European People's Party and the Portuguese Members of the Party
European public, witness the beginning of this process of accession, of the European Union joining the European Convention on Human Rights.

We, finally, a country like Portugal, which has consistently been at the forefront of human rights—abolishing slavery as early as 1761 in Portugal, and pioneering the abolition of the death penalty—can only naturally be interested in and supportive of this accession process.

I would, however, like to draw attention to the fact that we consider it extremely important for the European Parliament to closely follow every stage of the European Union's accession negotiations to the European Convention on Human Rights.

We consider this aspect, from a technical, legal, and political standpoint, to be fundamental, as Parliament is also a house of human rights and a place where human rights hold great significance. I would now like to simply emphasize what we consider to be very important.

For us members of the European Parliament, who maintain contact with many states outside the European Union, it is very important—it sends a very positive signal—that the European Union grants entry to the Council of Europe for its members who are not part of the European Union.
For these members of the Council of Europe, it is a sign of strengthened commitment by the European Union to their situation and to levels of democracy and respect for fundamental rights.
Madam President, Commissioner, social housing is an economic service of general interest with an important role in the market, generating positive externalities that benefit society as a whole.
According to Protocol 26, annexed to the Treaty of Lisbon, it is the responsibility of national, regional, and local authorities to carry out and organize this type of service.
Hence, this limitation of the target group to families with incomes below 33,000 euros is somewhat incomprehensible.

It is a measurement of poverty that is made in absolute and purely financial terms, failing to account for the diversity among the 27 member states, reducing the issue to a simple argument of solvency.
Now, social housing addresses cultural issues, social concerns, proximity to workplaces, the significant redefinition of women's role in the labor market, and worker mobility across European space.
The struggle the European Union is waging to overcome the current crisis hinges on economic growth, which in turn drives an increase in immigration and ethnic minorities.
economic and cultural aspects, and the need for their integration, even with the risks of ghettoization associated with it, which we must combat with just balance.
Is the Commission not encroaching upon the competence of the Member States?

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, Allow me, Madam President, to highlight the fact that we are discussing the first report from the Committee on Agriculture, subject to the new ordinary legislative procedure provided for by the Treaty of Lisbon.
This is a historic moment that, by confirming this Chamber's vote as I propose, will bestow a proper beginning to this new phase of sharing agricultural competences within the European Union.
I speak precisely with the Algeizolano about his overseas regions, as a practical demonstration of the willingness to build a Union that strengthens itself through cohesion.
in the spirit of active solidarity, respecting the specificities and differences among its parts, as the only viable path toward building our common European project.

I also speak by introducing essential regulation for the viability of the already century-old sugar industry in the Azores, the region I come from, which is why I particularly welcome this opportunity, thus contributing to a
healthy agricultural diversification in a region where, as we know, the dairy sector holds a unique, irreplaceable scale at the national level, which we must sustain and strengthen.
Madam President, I would like to thank Commissioner Ciúmes and the senior officials of the Commission with whom we have worked and negotiated for the openness and flexibility demonstrated.
The Spanish Presidency of the Council also made unrestrained efforts to turn our shared intention of reaching an agreement in first reading into reality.

This gratitude is, of course, extended to the shadow deputies of the other political groups, as well as to the excellent staff of the Secretariat of this Parliament's Committee on Agriculture, whose collaboration enabled us to reach a result that I consider highly positive.

I would also like to highlight the decisive contribution made by the Agricultural Federation of the Azores and by the Regional and Republic Governments in their involvement and commitment to securing a solution for the Azores sugar sector.

The presentation of the Council's draft regulation on October 2 before the Committee on Agriculture took place at a time when there were no prospects regarding the entry into force of the Lisbon Treaty, which continued to await ratification by all Member States.
In this context, the initial proposal of the Commission and the Council was that the draft be approved through a simplified procedure, without any amendments, to take effect as of 1 January 2010.

With the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon and despite, at the time, the prevailing opinion among other political groups, as well as the Council and the Commission, that the proposal should continue under the simplified procedure, as they believed that

The proposal did not require any amendments and should enter into force promptly. Although I personally held this view, my group and the Socialist and Democratic Group understood that Parliament should already express its position within the framework of the Treaty of Lisbon, fulfilling its role as co-legislator.

We did so at an opportune moment, because from then on new possibilities opened up, first through the contribution of the Committee on Regional Development and the opinion of colleague Nuno Teixeira, whom I take this opportunity to

to congratulate and thank for the cooperation always provided throughout this entire process, and then with my report introducing the new opportunities for sugar in the Azores, as well as with the contribution and support of my colleagues from the Committee on Agriculture.

I would like to say, finally, in this first intervention, that, regarding the amendments proposed by the Commission-Regi and voted on by the AGRI Committee and supported by me, we were unable to secure their acceptance in the compromise reached during the trilogue.

It was essential to secure agreement at first reading, thereby safeguarding the improvements already achieved and the need for entry into force without compromising its retroactivity from January of this year.
However, since the Commission is already preparing a review document for the ESIF, we will certainly have the opportunity to discuss them again shortly.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. We are facing an acceleration of a global change that is having devastating consequences for our economic, political, and social systems, and for all our citizens.
Today we witness an unprecedented weakening of states' capacity to respond.
The European Union consequently needs to identify common causes and allies and act clearly and unitedly on the world stage.
Extraordinary situations require clarity in joint action.

If we do not take the rigorous and collective accountability measures that are required, Europe will be condemned to marginalization and impoverishment. Only a strong Europe, respectful of its common rules, will be able to adequately respond to the new times.
Fragile and heavily indebted states lack the capacity to protect their citizens.
Because, if successful, we must be able to regain citizens' trust, win the battles of stability, budgetary discipline, job creation, monetary union stability, globalization, and strategic choices.
This, under penalty of jeopardizing our future.
And the future is secured by investing in social cohesion, ensuring peace, and building a new model based on the values of freedom, social justice, and responsibility.
Thus, the 2020 Strategy and the integrated Europe 2020 guidelines constitute essential tools for a new cycle of growth and employment in Europe.
For proper implementation and execution, it is necessary to establish clear and measurable objectives in employment, education, and poverty reduction.

It is also decisive for the success of the strategy that everything possible be done to facilitate the transposition by Member States of their national objectives and to ensure the proper implementation of this strategy. Many thanks.
When the Economic and Monetary Union celebrated its 10th anniversary, the Commission regarded the Euro as the Union's most successful project.
Today we would have difficulty asserting this without reservations, because the crisis has clearly demonstrated that the Economic and Monetary Union is an unfinished project.
Minimum rescue measures were taken in an emergency atmosphere, but let us not deceive ourselves. The conditions of pressure, the direct interests of the main...
member countries. The painful nature of the decisions and their slow pace weakened the image of the euro and the European Union in the eyes of citizens and the world.
We must face the facts.
The European Union and the Euro are a process of integration, not merely intergovernmental cooperation.
They are an unfinished process that at this moment either strengthens or faces serious risks of disappearing.
There are three themes that must be addressed. First, it is important to consolidate the instruments that have been launched in this climate of emergency. A monetary fund must be created.

European, it is necessary to advance on eurobonds, it is necessary to advance on the architecture of European supervision and regulation, and it is necessary to advance on a European dimension in the management of banking crises.
Second dimension: it is important to recognize that there is no strong currency if the economy is weak. The 1% growth projected for the European Union is unsustainable in terms of...
both in terms of its demographic composition and in combating unemployment. The 2020 Strategy is no more than a set of good ideas if it lacks the means for implementation.

Third dimension, Mr. Commissioner Ollirane: fiscal consolidation is important, but it cannot coexist with growing divergences among the Member States comprising the Union. The convergence policy must be urgently revisited. Thank you very much, Mr. President.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Several colleagues here from the PPF have already provided a very comprehensive overview of the situation.
I believe that now it will be necessary to demand and to make reference to the role of the Nation States.
In fact, national governments also have a role here in enforcing requirements within their respective states.
and the conditions necessary for the single currency to be strengthened and for this project of monetary and economic integration to move forward.
And here I would like to say that our party, the party linked to the PPE, which is the PSD in Portugal, is supporting the Government's austerity measures, because we consider

that the mistakes made by the Socialist Government in Portugal over these past 15 years, which unfortunately are now evident to all, need to be corrected, and that the PSD will provide support for these corrections to be made.

But if we are supporting austerity measures, we also criticize the Portuguese government. And it serves as a good example for other similar cases, because it is not actually reducing expenditure. And what is essential is not merely...

increase taxes to reduce the deficit, but rather reduce expenditure. It is demanding that the Government must have the courage to take measures that cut spending, ease the deficit, and alleviate public debt.
This applies to Portugal and also to other countries that are currently in a difficult situation. And we, as European parliamentarians, have the responsibility to also do so in our own countries.
Apology for the necessary measures to ensure the euro emerges strengthened, and to enable the realization of this space of integration and this dream— a dream of the PPE and a dream of us all.
Therefore, we criticize those governments that lack the courage to take the necessary measures within their own states.

Dear colleagues, I would like to begin by saying that, in our opinion, the report on the evaluation of the results of the 2006–2010 action plan for gender equality, which aims to influence future strategies on this matter, could have been more precise and less controversial.

In fact, the approach to several ethically sensitive issues—particularly the issue of women's sexual and reproductive health—should have been different if the true objective had been to achieve the broadest possible consensus.

That said, I would like to emphasize that during periods of so-called normal economic growth, concern regarding women's rights—particularly the disparities between men and women in the workplace, society, and the family—is significant. However, these issues are profoundly exacerbated by an economic and social crisis such as the one we are experiencing.
Hence the need for a strengthened strategic commitment by the Member States, at the level of local and regional authorities and community institutions, regarding protection

to say that, in line with what President Daul and colleague Werner Langen stated, it is essential that there be an Economic Government of the Union, but that this Economic Government should be centered on the Commission.
This is the first point I wished to emphasize. And having said that, I would add that we should not merely, as has been done throughout this morning here, focus our debate on immediate issues,
also in those related to the 2020 Strategy. I would particularly emphasize the issues of education and training for young people, as well as innovation, research, and development.

Because these are the niches that will enable Europe to remain competitive in the global arena. And today I must say, regretfully, that the European educational model has failed. It has failed because, when compared to the models of emerging countries,

Like India, like China, like Brazil, it is today a less demanding and less rigorous model. And therefore, I also ask that in the boldness of this idea of innovation, development, research, and education, there be rigor and high standards within the context of European educational systems.
Only with this rigor and high standards do we prepare our youth to compete in the global market.

We cannot focus solely on this year's crisis or the next two or three years; we must think in terms of a strategy for Europe over the next 10 to 20 years, and this depends on an education system based on rigor and high standards.
Mr. Commissioner, never before has the European project, built with such effort over the past 50 years, been so threatened.

The frustration of European citizens is immense. The financial system, which was meant to serve them, has destroyed their savings, their pensions, their investment plans, their jobs, in short, their future.
Regarding the necessary strengthening of the entire financial services market—banks, insurance, securities—the path to follow is clear and lies in the architecture we are discussing here today.
We actually need a strong European supervisory architecture that covers all products and market participants, and is equipped with methodologies and instruments to
effective intervention and capable of responding to the reality of the market, which in particular, within the European context, does not recognize national borders.

Risk cannot and should not be eliminated from the financial market, but we do need regulation that makes this risk more transparent and prevents the accumulation of bubbles whose burst would affect the entire system and spill over into the real economy.

But if, despite these safeguards, new crises occur in the future, it is crucial to have mechanisms in place that limit contagion, particularly with regard to the banking system, as it is vital for the functioning of the economy and the guardian of citizens' savings.
Here lies, precisely within the powers and competences of the banking authority, the ability to implement a new strategy for managing cross-border banking crises—a subject addressed in my report.

It is not within our power to prevent the failure of banks, but rather to ensure that their restructuring or eventual liquidation is carried out in a manner that safeguards the stability of the financial system and protects the interests of all stakeholders.
ordered form, limiting side effects on the rest of the system and, above all, preventing taxpayers from bearing the costs of crises they generate.

To achieve this, a set of new devices is required. It is necessary to adopt common rules, definitions, methodologies, instruments, and terminology for all parties involved in restructuring, reorganization, or liquidation operations.
It is necessary to harmonize the relevant national legal frameworks for crisis management, particularly bankruptcy and liquidation laws.
It is necessary to grant supervisors broad powers of intervention.
It is necessary to establish clear criteria, not only quantitative but also qualitative, that enable interventions to be initiated in an institution facing difficulties.
Contingency plans need to be prepared.
All this process for 27 member states requires time.
However, approximately 50 banks out of the 12,000 operating in Europe account for 70% of the assets.
Now, this means an enormous concentration of risk, but also an opportunity to intervene prioritarily on them.

There are, therefore, conditions justifying the proposal of a special and urgent process for resolving banks that, due to their cross-border nature, size, and relationships with other banks, may have systemic implications for the financial system.
Now, for these banks, it is possible to anticipate a special supervision by the new European Banking Authority, although operating within the College of National Supervisors,
It is possible and urgent that they, given the risk they pose, begin contributing to a fund that finances potential interventions.
that are necessary, and it is important that the banking authority establish a specialized unit to coordinate interventions in the event of difficulties in these banks.
Overall, the proposed elements ensure that interventions in systemically significant transnational banks are consistent and coherent among
different countries, and above all, that a principle so cherished in Europe be fulfilled: that the costs of pollution caused by these banks be borne by the polluter themselves.
This proposal fits perfectly within what we are discussing, particularly regarding the competences of the banking authority. I would even say it is the most obvious test of its effectiveness.
In the case of the vote in the Economic and Monetary Committee, there was a very large majority in support of this proposal, 87% of the votes.
We also count here in the plenary with the overwhelming vote of this institution, in the sense that, by strongly supporting this visionary, realistic, yet pragmatic proposal, we...
Let us take an action that is urgent and necessary to rekindle Europeans' hope in Europe, in their financial system, and in particular in the banking system. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much.
The first is that it was an extraordinary experience working in such a collaborative manner with my colleagues from the other political forces.
Thank you very much for including me in the supervision package, and in particular, thank you to Mr. Garcia Margalho for all the collaboration that was possible.
Secondly, I would like to thank my personal collaborators as well as my colleagues who contributed to the specific report entrusted to me, which enabled me to incorporate 90% of their proposals.
A special word for the Secretariat, for Susana Vravová, and for the services of the Commission.

Finally, my word of thanks to the Commissioner, his services, and the very clear expression of hope that, indeed, on the basis of the report I hope will be approved tomorrow, it will be possible to build a solid foundation—a truly European one—that safeguards citizens.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner. I would also like to begin by thanking the rapporteurs for this excellent work.

Deforestation, as we know, is one of the planet's main environmental and social problems, and each year we lose 13 million hectares of forest, thus contributing to 20% of global carbon emissions and posing a significant threat to biodiversity.

The contribution of deforestation to climate change is undeniable and, as such, it is highly responsible for the occurrence of extreme weather events, floods, and even the desertification of vast areas of the planet.
Forest loss endangers all of humanity, but this risk and its impact are not shared equally among us.
Communities with fewer resources and those that rely most heavily on forests and nature for survival are the most affected.
Deforestation concerns our collective existence, the scarcity of natural resources, their distribution, the rights of local and indigenous communities, and social justice.
The main cause of deforestation is illegal logging, which accounts for 20 to 40 percent of global industrial wood production.
Therefore, we are also discussing the transfer of wealth from wood-producing countries to wood-consuming countries.
This illegal practice jeopardizes the sustainable exploitation of wood and the companies that engage in it.
Words alone are not enough.
This Parliament must take strong and decisive measures in this fight.
This report takes a very significant step in this direction by establishing a ban on the trade of timber.
Good intentions alone are not enough either.
Effective means are required, namely sanctions that in this case could take three forms.

First, fines that effectively eliminate the economic benefit derived from illegal practices, increased in cases of recidivism. Second, confiscation of the timber from the products in question. And third, immediate suspension of the authorization to carry out a commercial activity.

And this is so because those who exploit or trade a natural resource should be socially responsible. This is also how it should be that those placing wood or wood products on the European Union market for the first time should be required to exercise due diligence to ensure the wood was harvested legally.
That is why all subsequent operators must provide basic information about this wood, namely its origin and its destination.
The public must also have the right— and I conclude here, Mr. President—the right to access information about who is complying and who is not complying. Thank you very much.

For the celebration, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, the objective of territorial cohesion, now enshrined in the Treaty of Lisbon, embodies the value of European solidarity and the commitment of the Member States and the Union to reduce existing disparities among the various regions.

There are regions within the Union that face persistent constraints that strongly condition their economic and social development.

That is why it is necessary to implement specific programs that effectively and genuinely overcome the lag in these regions by promoting an adaptation of their development model to their potentials and resources, and contributing to achieving the objectives of the EU 2020 strategy.

This incentive for these regions should stem not only from the instruments of regional policy, through its structural adjustment mechanisms—such as the Cohesion Fund and the four structural funds—but also
as well as from a different use of various sectoral policies, which have a significant territorial impact in each region and can therefore have a stimulating effect on their economies.
For the determination of European assistance to these regions, it is certain that we must continue to use GDP as the primary eligibility indicator.

Nevertheless, and taking into account its natural disadvantages, it becomes pertinent to resort to other measurable criteria capable of providing a more accurate picture of its level of development, such as the unemployment rate, population density, or education level.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I would like to begin by congratulating my colleagues Luan and Manescu on the outstanding work they have carried out in their respective reports.
Both constitute an important contribution to the ongoing discussion about the new cohesion policy for the 2014–2020 framework.
Today, more than ever, achieving economic, social, and territorial cohesion is essential for affirming the European Union's competitiveness on a global scale.
This goal will only be achieved if we recognize that strengthening and developing the regional dimension is the only way to ensure a cohesive Europe internally and a competitive Europe externally.

In this regard, I would highlight three points that I consider fundamental. First, decentralization, ensuring that local and regional authorities, particularly those with legislative powers, participate more actively and contribute to a more effective implementation of the cohesion policy.

Member States are encouraged to invite regional and local authorities to participate in negotiations regarding the future of structural funds, on equal footing with national authorities and representatives. And lastly, strengthening the role of regional authorities in the preparation, management, and implementation of programs.
Only in this way, with the fullest involvement of these authorities throughout the entire process, will the principle of subsidiarity be fulfilled.

Another aspect I consider crucial is the request for a simpler architecture for post-2013 funds, not as a consequence of the economic crisis, but as a general principle of the future cohesion policy, in order to facilitate the absorption of funds.
The underlying concern behind the Treaty of Lisbon was to bring Europe closer to its citizens.
Let us therefore do not only this, but also make your life easier by avoiding unnecessary administrative burdens that discourage your participation.
Only in this way will we be able to achieve the goal of genuine territorial cohesion
Only in this way will we see reduced the disparities within the European Union, which today are markedly contrasted and feature a vast gap between rich and poor regions.

Greater involvement also means greater responsibility. It is this responsibility that must be embraced and shared by all, doing more and better in order to achieve a stronger and more competitive Europe.

During those days, the least we can say is that an arbitrary regime prevailed in Portugal, a genuine police force of thought.
A regime so particular that it prompted the Portuguese government to purchase anti-riot equipment worth five million euros, and which did not even arrive in time for the summit.
And these are the priorities, after all, of a government facing a major social crisis.
In the Europe we dream of, freedom of expression is a fundamental value.
The Europe we dream of does not bow before military organizations that write their history with blood.
The NATO summit produced the legitimization of its military intervention around the globe.
The subalternization of the European Union to American military strategy and interests also emerged.
Thirdly, the militarization of access to energy sources was consolidated.
Lastly, the military defense of the development model that generates environmental imbalances and disrupts social crisis was established.
Therefore, Madam President, I wish to say that, in my view, with this summit, we have lost in democracy, we have lost in freedom, and we have lost so that some may profit from war.

And there are several factors that may explain this increase, the first of which is a short-term factor,
It has to do with the coincidence of poor weather conditions in certain countries, such as Russia and Argentina,
But there are two factors that are structuring, of a structural nature, and that is precisely why I bring these two factors here.

to state that, in the case of the PSD, fully integrated into the EPP, there is absolute and total willingness to meet those requirements and obligations arising from integration into the European space.

Unlike what the Socialist Government has done, the Social Democratic Party has shown full willingness to provide the guarantees to Europe that may be necessary in the context of this crisis.

And with this, I wish to give this signal, without bringing internal politics into the European Parliament, of the Social Democratic Party's commitment and full guarantee regarding the strict agenda the country will need to follow after its elections on June 5.
Thank you very much, Madam President, dear colleagues, Commissioner Stefan Fuller. The historic moment we are living through demands firm and determined action.
Like many of you, we observe the events unfolding in the Mediterranean Basin with a mixture of hope, concern, and anticipation.

I feel, nonetheless, satisfied with the proactive stance that Parliament is preparing to adopt regarding the review of the European Union's southern neighbourhood policy, which I hope will result in a stronger presence of the Union in this region—not merely as a financier, but equally as a committed partner.
From the outset, by affirming the absolute necessity of defending our foundational values in relations with neighboring countries.
We can no longer compromise in defending democracy, nor in defending human rights, and least of all in defending social justice.

We can no longer prioritize short-term stability at the expense of the higher interests of citizens and their steadfast defense of individual and collective freedom, with particular attention to women's rights.
In the future, the Union must prioritize a bottom-up approach in its neighborhood policy.
It is essential that this takes place.
Only the greater involvement of local communities and civil society will ensure maximum effectiveness in its implementation.

But I cannot refrain from expressing a sense of frustration. I regret that this Parliament and the Commission are unwilling to seize this opportunity to clearly distinguish the eastern neighborhood policy, aimed at countries that may potentially become our partners in the Union in the future, from the southern neighborhood policy.
I also challenge the Commission to demonstrate, in its 20 April review process, the ambition that the current moment demands: a policy of neighbourhood.
custom-made to suit each state, with clearer assets and a careful assessment that enables the creation of a future Mediterranean economic space alongside the new democracies of the South.

To conclude, Mr. Commissioner, we trust that the positive atmosphere and cooperation that have characterized the work on this subject will lead to the European Parliament's sustained involvement in the planning and evaluation of this policy. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. The reports we have discussed, and which today's rapporteurs, whom I congratulate, reinforce, constitute a set of initiatives whose ambition is to relaunch the internal market.
I would like to highlight the commitment of the European Commission, particularly that of Commissioner Barnier, whom I welcome, and the positive reception given by this Parliament through the
Consumer Protection and Internal Market Committee, to prioritize competitiveness and market dynamism, a fundamental pillar of the 2020 Strategy.
Citizens and businesses expect effective stimuli for Europe to resume economic growth, they anticipate job creation, and aim for greater prosperity.

And, as Commissioner Mr. highlighted, opening markets to our companies by eliminating barriers and difficulties will provide a particularly significant boost and an unparalleled opportunity, enabling them to continue their mission as the driving force of our economy.

Measures aimed at reducing the administrative and bureaucratic burden on SMEs, and improving their access to credit and the services market, the European patent, and the recognition of qualifications, are essential.
the relevance of e-commerce development in strengthening consumer and business confidence in this type of commerce, the emphasis on innovation
crucial for strong and sustainable growth. Social entrepreneurship is essential for developing innovative business projects aimed at social inclusion.
These are some measures that will ensure the successful completion, deepening, and full exploitation of the single market for the benefit of European citizens.
Finally, the key recommendation that Member States intensify their efforts to improve the transposition and implementation of internal market rules. Many thanks.
I also wish to join my voice to all those who have expressed concerns about the low incomes of fishermen, exacerbated by the historically high fuel prices reached, particularly in recent months.
As has been well stated here, fuel costs have a very significant impact on the survival of our small and medium-sized shipowners, particularly exacerbating the already fragile profits of fishing.
This gradual rise in prices is also bringing us profound negative effects: reduced fishing trips to sea, meager incomes for fishermen, worsened by the low prices received at first sale,

increases in imports from third countries of aquaculture and fisheries products to the European market and a consequent abandonment of the activity due to financial difficulties, such as loss of jobs.
In this context, to prevent the ongoing worsening of the crisis, it is necessary to act—acting with solidarity and addressing the problems of fishing with depth.

The Commission must therefore provide positive responses through short- and long-term measures to address the hardships facing the sector and the serious income losses being experienced by our fishermen.
First of all, I would like to particularly commend here the work of our PPE colleague, Mário David, whom I am representing today, as he is thoroughly informed.
Unable to attend this plenary session, I would like to say that we have always considered supporting the victims of the floods in Pakistan to be an ethical and moral imperative.
And for this reason, we strongly support all measures taken in the direction of traditional humanitarian aid.
We even consider that work should continue toward helping stabilize the situation of so many millions of affected people.
What we cannot accept is that the European Union's trade policy is now regarded as an appropriate and suitable instrument for humanitarian aid.

The truth is that this brings two detrimental consequences. One consequence within the European Union, particularly for Southern countries and their textile and apparel industry, today highly weakened by an economic, social, and financial crisis unparalleled since the

And secondly, at the global level, in WTO negotiations, the opening of the world's largest trading bloc, the European Union, to potential Pakistani exports could completely disrupt the WTO negotiations.
And it is no coincidence that they are at the impasse in which they find themselves.

It is therefore natural that, in the face of such a situation, we should have to say, regarding the history of this Regulation and the difficulties it encountered during these eight months of intense negotiations, for the

which we have collaborated on the most constructive proposals, we now unfortunately have to take a position that will be one in support of the amendment, which in effect foresees the end of this regulatory proposal as we know it.
We will have to do it at this moment, not out of less solidarity with Pakistan, but because we believe humanitarian aid should be delivered in the appropriate place and in the appropriate manner.
Mr. President, Commissioner, I would like to begin by saying that, naturally, I consider the agreement reached for Portugal to be a balanced one.
It is a very harsh agreement for the populations, but it fundamentally focuses on structural reforms aimed at increasing growth and competitiveness.
And I believe this is very positive.

I do not adopt here the stance of others who believe that countries such as Greece, Ireland, and Portugal did everything correctly, made no mistakes, exaggerated nothing, did everything well, and that it was solely the international crisis that caused the problems.

No. Our governments have made mistakes and pursued wrong policies. But this does not mean that there is not now an opportunity within the context of the Union to once again have the possibility of returning to the right path and the correct course.

And therefore, it is important to emphasize here that, in fact, one must tell public opinion, which opposes supporting countries in difficulty, that they are not actually giving money to these countries, but merely lending money to them.
but they are lending them at very high interest rates, and therefore are conducting a transaction that is beneficial for their budgets and their populations.

But I also say here that perhaps it is time to have a comprehensive European response, including the introduction of eurobonds and the possibility of issuing them in the primary market to address international markets.

It might also be the right moment for the European Commission, the Fund, and the IMF to review their interest rates, so as to enable sustained growth in these countries currently facing difficulties by lowering those rates.

Thank you. I now give the floor to Mr. Correia de Campos for one minute. Thank you very much, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, and Madam Rapporteur. This strategy is important for all European countries, especially those whose economies lack greater competitiveness.
It is essential for the creation of small and medium-sized enterprises.
Today, the generation of new jobs is more active.
It is essential for universities and laboratories whose survival depends in part on their relationship with productive sectors.
It is essential for the financial system to find in innovation a new market, as the traditional manufacturing model diminishes.

Innovation is at the top of the European agenda, and as a cornerstone of the EU 2020 strategy, it will require new financial instruments to prevent it from being undermined by temporary budgetary constraints.
I would finally like to welcome Judite Merckes for her important contribution to a more innovative Europe and an economy more based on knowledge. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, Madam President, Madam Commissioner, I would like to begin by congratulating my colleague Odi on his excellent report.

The interim evaluation, although late, enabled highlighting the critical aspects of the seventh programme. Its excessive bureaucracy, the industry's lack of interest, the number and complexity of the new instruments, and the slow pace of disbursements,
There remains a greater risk of funds being transferred from the current framework programme to large-scale programmes of questionable added value.

but also allowed highlighting positive aspects, greater international articulation and cooperation among scientific groups in joint projects, some successful cases—including the European Research Council—and a greater gender balance.
There are priorities that must be addressed. First, simplification. Second, promoting scientific excellence—not in some countries, but across all of Europe. A stronger connection between

a framework programme on innovation, with instruments targeted at small and medium-sized enterprises and entrepreneurship, of which the most successful is the Eurostar programme, which should be expanded. Thank you, sir.

Madam President, Commissioner, I am addressing directly the proposal you have presented here today to Parliament, and I must say frankly that I believe we, some of us, have the right to feel disappointed by this proposal, because the proposal put forward is not only weak but also riddled with serious gaps.
I believe this is a European issue, not one dividing states with nuclear industries from those without; it concerns all of Europe, and Europe desperately needs good news.
And I must honestly tell you that one of these good news items is ensuring citizens that we are taking care of their safety.
Now, when these tests are carried out as the Commissioner has presented them—that is, a first phase conducted by the interested parties themselves, a second phase carried out by the Member States—

These interested parties defend their positions, and a third phase is carried out by peers who are genuinely interested. I wonder whether the outcome will ultimately amount to saying that all nuclear power plants are in very good health and therefore recommended.

We know it is not the case, and therefore I sincerely hope there will be serious consequences, so that we can tell our citizens that we are truly safeguarding their security, and not merely pretending to do so. Thank you very much.
I expect this European Council, especially given the climate prevailing in recent weeks, to make a decision of great responsibility.
And greater responsibility than that which existed at the previous Council, where an issue was merely postponed for three months due to electoral calendars.

I hope, from this decision on responsibility, above all, that it becomes clear that economic and financial issues are crucial and decisive for Europe, but that these economic and financial matters are instrumental to the principal objective of Europe, which is to maintain peace.
If an approach to the problems is made that leads solely to States beginning to distrust one another, to instilling distrust among the different States,
to create and exacerbate differences and rivalries between Southern and Northern States, between central and peripheral States. What we will be led to is the decline of the European Union.

I believe that what is at stake in this European Council, regardless of technical solutions or political disagreements, is whether or not we want the European project to remain a valid instrument for Europe's peace strategy.
I swear that this is what is at stake.
What I notice in many speeches by leaders, both those from countries facing difficulties and those from countries that are economic payers,
taxpayers, it is an irresponsible discourse, which often appeals to some characteristics of nationalities that are extremely negative.
I appeal for this, and only this, by feeling the responsibility of the leaders of the European governments to find solutions that lead Europe out of the crisis and toward a resolution.

Thank you very much, Madam President, Commissioner. My first words are to commend the work carried out by the rapporteur, my colleague Jorge Sravacakis, who has done exceptional work in identifying future synergies for enhanced effectiveness among the various structural funds.

Your report also contributes to this joint debate on cohesion policy, a discussion that assumes growing importance, not only because of the substance of the topic, but also and above all due to the timing of its consideration.
Indeed, we are currently in the process of examining the Commission's proposal, not only regarding the new financial perspectives but also concerning the future cohesion policy beyond 2013.
Cohesion policy is a fundamental policy for the European Union.

It is a horizontal policy that, through its primary instruments—the structural funds—finances projects and programs in the Member States, aiming to reduce the disparities between the development levels of various regions.
Experience, however, has shown us the difficulties in terms of efficiency and outcomes resulting from the fragmentation and complexity of this policy.

I propose that a single strategic framework for the various funds, based on an integrated, results-oriented approach, is the starting point for greater and better synergies among the different instruments and for enhancing their effectiveness.
It is also essential for better coordination among various sectoral policies and for improved efficiency in the use of resources.
We must seize the danger of crisis to seek new alternatives.

And it is certain that all these reports currently under discussion here will present us with enormous challenges. We know that to meet these challenges, we also need a sound budget. But we must make a clear distinction between budgetary aspects and political objectives.
The political objectives are well known. And indeed, only by asserting these political objectives can we offer a future to the regions.

And if this is indeed the case, then we must place the budget at the service of the future of regions and citizens, rather than placing citizens and regions at the service of the budget. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Mr. President. Cohesion policy is the key policy for the future of the European project.
The establishment of a common space, with an internal market and a shared currency, requires a certain level of convergence among its member states and regions in order to function effectively.
Current competitiveness disparities and development asymmetries have inevitably led to the weakening of the European space and to the crisis affecting the eurozone.
This is why this vital need for convergence absolutely requires a strong cohesion budget that cannot be weakened to finance other policies.
The European Union, to do more, must have more. And this is achieved by growing its budget, not by reducing it—even skillfully—particularly the cohesion budget.

I ask the Commissioner whether the establishment of the Infrastructure Fund and the New Innovation Policies, which will be linked to cohesion and regional development, can, without an increase in the community budget, become the Trojan horse that introduces new expenditures into the cohesion budget.

Modernization and renewal of energy infrastructure are essential factors for achieving the sustainable and smart growth objectives we pursue under the 2020 Strategy and for our energy security, which we require.
It is essential to involve all regions in this challenge.

To achieve this, the incentives provided by regulatory systems must be improved, particularly those applicable to higher-risk projects and more innovative initiatives, such as those being developed in the Azores islands in the field of renewable energy.

Regions should be associated with the conception and implementation phases of projects, in defining priorities, as well as in their preparation and execution, since this ensures better outcomes and thus a stronger defense of the European interest.
The current economic situation further underscores the need to adopt an innovative and integrated approach to energy issues, taking into account all economic, environmental, and social aspects.
It is essential not to lose sight of its medium- and long-term effects, while striving to ensure all European citizens have access to safe, sustainable, and affordable energy.

The report we have discussed is of particular importance given the influence that general interest social services have on the daily lives of European citizens, as well as on social cohesion, job creation, and economic growth.

Collaboration with rapporteur Próncias de Roça was highly beneficial and clarifying, as the rapporteur himself just stated, enabling the adoption of several compromises within the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs.
Given the outcome we currently have here and are discussing, I congratulate and commend the rapporteur.
In fact, on our part, the main concern was to make clear that responsibilities in this matter lie with the Member States.
National authorities are entrusted with the responsibilities of defining, organizing, and financing social services of general interest.
Thus, there is no need for the creation of harmonizing legislation at the European level, given the great diversity of these general interest social services across the 27 Member States.

Just consider health services, social security, employment services, and social housing—these are examples demonstrating that proximity and knowledge of local contexts are fundamental to the effective and fair delivery of such services. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, as rapporteur for the EESC opinion on the future of social and international services, I would like to congratulate my colleague from Roça, highlighting the fact that we are facing an essential pillar of the social market economy model we advocate for Europe, in which the SIG plays a fundamental role in ensuring its cohesion.

Social cohesion at the level of the European Union advises against overlooking the existence of minimum protection levels in all Member States, without prejudice to respect for the competences of their national, regional, and local authorities.

European regions play a very important role in providing these services to populations, and their views should be taken into account, given the inherent constraints they face, in order to ensure the provision of these services to the populations.

In a period of crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing, Social Work and General Intensity constitutes a social protection network for the most disadvantaged or for those whose incomes do not allow them access to private services.

Thus, it is important to ensure that member states, when decentralizing powers, also provide the corresponding financial resources, so that regional and local authorities can effectively deliver services to the populations.
Madam Ashton, I begin by saying it is obvious that we must continue to support the democratic revolutions taking place in these countries.
I don't have much time, so I just want to leave a few notes.
First of all, we must not forget that the conditions for negotiation, aimed at a democratic transition rather than civil war, must be defined by the peoples living in these countries.
The situation in Syria is serious. This applies to Syria, but it also applies to Yemen and Bahrain.

If we wish to avoid civil war, the last thing these countries need is for Europe to interfere in their internal affairs, and especially, and very particularly, through the imposition of military or paramilitary solutions.
And if I say this, I say it because we must totally reject what is currently happening in Libya.
NATO and the United Nations have exceeded all resolutions, even those adopted by the United Nations Security Council.
It is this silence regarding religiosity, this rejection that we must make, and it is precisely because it is not included in this resolution that I cannot endorse it. Many thanks.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. President of the Commission, Mr. Prime Minister. First of all, I would naturally like to congratulate the Polish Presidency for the ambitious programme it has brought here, and in particular, to commend it for...

neighborhood policy, not only to the east, but also along the southern border, where I believe a challenge is currently unfolding that may be more demanding than the one we face to the east, which is longer-term and less immediate.

But, having said that, I would like to ask the Prime Minister and the Polish Presidency to pay particular attention to economic integration policies, economic governance, and financial market supervision.
I believe that at this moment, however ambitious our goals may be in areas such as energy security, food security, the environment, agriculture, and multiannual financial frameworks, if we do not

If we do not resolve in a clear and solid manner the problem of the debt crisis within the European context, we will not have an Europe strong enough to address these challenges that the Polish presidency has taken on.

And that is why I drew attention to the fact that it is essential to convince the remaining heads of state and heads of government that greater economic integration and greater economic governance are needed for the European Union.

Without this, what will happen is that, instead of being the drivers or, at the very least, the representatives of our peoples, whether in the Council, in the Parliament, or in the Commission for European Interests, instead of being that, we will merely be...
Mr. President, as Chair of the MASHREC Delegation, I would like to share a sentence about each of my four countries, naturally enclosed in quotation marks.
Starting with Syria and reiterating what some of my colleagues have already stated, I regret that the international community appears, to public opinion, to be applying double standards.

It is necessary to denounce, if required, the countries in the Security Council—obviously Russia and China, and besides Brazil, though with veto power—that have prevented the international community from adopting a more unified stance in line with the values we share.
With regard to Egypt, there is an urgent need to raise awareness among Egyptian authorities about postponing the elections, as has already been done in Tunisia.
As for Lebanon, I believe it is essential that this debate send a very clear message to the new Lebanese government.
so that they are aware they will be judged according to their conduct in relation to the charges brought by the Special Tribunal for Lebanon.

Regarding Jordan, it is an example that a peaceful transition is possible, and perhaps all that is needed at this stage is to raise awareness and encourage the authorities to accelerate the reform process.
We must not, of course, overlook in a debate of this nature, the peace process in the Middle East. And reiterate our hope that the upcoming meeting of the Quartet on Monday may
effectively be productive and reaffirm that this House would certainly welcome much greater involvement by the European Union in the entire process.

I have no doubt that there will be a new round of negotiations before the United Nations General Assembly in September. But I hope that this round of negotiations will be productive and conducted in good faith.
Thank you, Mr. President. I begin by congratulating the rapporteur and all those who contributed to achieving this work.
There is no doubt that this report constitutes and will constitute a reference point regarding the recommendations and solid, coordinated responses to the challenges posed by the crisis.
If there is one conclusion that can be drawn, it is that the crisis we are experiencing will only be overcome through the deepening of European integration.

In a period marked by severe financial, economic, and social difficulties, and painful austerity measures in some Member States, such as in my country, Portugal, there is a temptation to retreat into protectionist and nationalist attitudes.
But that is a mistake.

The European response to the crisis requires a genuine European economic governance that pursues sustainable growth, promotes employment creation, and develops the social market economy, providing long-term solutions with clearly defined objectives that member states can identify with.
The strategy for exiting the crisis needs to be financed with a sufficiently ambitious community budget.
To prevent a new sovereign debt and euro crisis, closer coordination in fiscal policy is required, along with the establishment of a public debt issuance system and eurobonds.
I conclude by emphasizing that during this difficult period, it is also necessary to maintain levels of social protection, particularly for the most vulnerable citizens. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, Commissioner, Presidency in office, it is common to say that crises help strengthen Europe.
Historically this was the case, but today I am no longer so certain that this remains true.

The slow, improvised, reactive approach, as the Union is responding to the Greek crisis, worsens the situation every day and spreads fear and lack of confidence in the Union and its currency in a frightening manner.
This despite the problems of the eurozone stemming from obvious flaws in the construction of the single currency, which today focus on its most vulnerable links.

For the current crisis to strengthen the Union, a strategic vision is required—one that existed in the past and unfortunately neither the Commission nor the Council has managed to impart to the European programme and project.
This Parliament's report, however, gives us this perspective: more Europe, not less, is the solution.
The Parliament recalls that the market and the single currency generate unbearable divergences among economies and that these must be counteracted by specific policies.

It acknowledges that the functioning of the euro requires a strengthened European budget backed by own resources, and calls for a European debt issuance agency capable of protecting member states from market speculation.
and provide them with credit at reasonable prices and terms. It also acknowledges that diplomas and the mobility of workers between countries require mutual recognition of social rights and pensions.
The Parliament has united to send clear messages. Let us hope the Commission and the Council hear them.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, dear rapporteur colleague, I would first like to naturally congratulate and commend the rapporteur colleague, as I believe this report is extremely useful and indeed enables us to take new steps forward in the field of mediation.
