The report provides an overview, a range of solutions adopted by the various member states in implementing, applying the
Directive 2008-52-EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 21 May 2008, naturally relating to mediation in civil and commercial matters.
Now, we are all aware of the advantages of mediation.
On one hand, it is a judicial mechanism for resolving conflicts, contributes to relieving court congestion, rationalizes the justice system, and simultaneously provides
simultaneously providing the parties with the opportunity to achieve a friendly, faster, and less expensive resolution, thereby reducing the burden of the litigation in which they are engaged.

That is why it is essential to reaffirm the objective of Article 1 of the Directive when calling for the adoption of incentive measures regarding the use of alternative dispute resolution methods, and in particular mediation.
Promoting access to and use of extrajudicial conflict reduction mechanisms contributes to improving, simplifying, and making our justice system more accessible.

That is why it poses a very important challenge for the European Union and for the establishment of the area of freedom, security, and justice, I would say, for the concept of the rule of law within the European Union.
And that is why, in this context of crisis as well, I consider it very important—just as stated in the report—that public opinion and operators be made aware
judicial bodies on this issue and appeal here to the Commission and to the Commissioner for the prompt presentation of a legislative proposal on alternative dispute resolution.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner. I would also like to emphasize the fundamental importance of this report regarding the transparency of the European energy market.
And I would simply like to say that this is the central domain of cooperation in European terms, provided we safeguard certain fundamental dimensions, among them avoiding that the
liberalization turns into higher energy prices for citizens, ensure equal access, and exercise caution regarding sanctions, as already mentioned by numerous colleagues.

And precisely because this is a central domain of European cooperation, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, I cannot help but refer to it and challenge you, and ask you directly, Mr. Commissioner, face to face,
I would like to give you the opportunity to explain your recent statements regarding Greece, Ireland, and Portugal, as these are also matters of European cooperation.
Symbolic measures such as lowering the flags to half-mast, Mr. Commissioner, only intensify the shame felt by citizens who are worthy and who strive for a Europe

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. Chief Commissioner for Covites, esteemed Representatives of the Council, Polish Presidency. First of all, I would like to clarify that I will be speaking here in a dual capacity. On one hand, replacing the author of the opinion of the Committee on Affairs

Constitutional matters, my colleague Messer Schmidt, the Danish representative who is absent as the election process in Denmark is currently underway, and therefore I am representing him both as a member from Africa and also as a member of the Jury, serving as shadow rapporteur for the Jury on this report.
The first point I would like to make is that, in the crisis we are currently experiencing—the economic and financial crisis—the issue of smart regulation, or better legislation, is a central concern.

There is no doubt that, in the so-called structural reforms that can kick-start the economic and financial framework of the European space, improvement at the level of legislation and regulation is absolutely essential.

It is evident that a Europe aiming, on the one hand, to be dynamic and competitive must have simple and accessible regulation for businesses, and likewise, obviously, for citizens. It must therefore be oriented toward their needs.
The truth is that, in this regard, it is essential today that, first of all, the program for reducing administrative burdens be achieved, and that all possible efforts be made toward

promote consultations with market actors, representative associations of citizens and businesses, and conduct impact assessments of the legislation itself—this is also extremely important, so that the

the decision-making process could essentially be based, say, on the views of those who are the intended recipients of the legislation, and could be carried out without administrative burdens and with lower economic and financial costs.

It is important to note that, for the Committee on Constitutional Affairs in particular, the concept of proportionality and subsidiarity is relevant—that idea that European intervention should be appropriate and not one that promotes the complexity of the legal system.

And therefore, in our view, we have paid particular attention to the enhanced role that National Parliaments play in this dynamic, precisely by virtue of the principles of proportionality and subsidiarity.

That is why it is essential that, at both the level of Smart Regulation and the improvement of legislation, we now involve National Parliaments. So that, whether at the European level or the national level, both legislation and regulation

be more agile, more intelligent, more efficient. And therefore, this is an excellent moment to put into practice what we announce every day: a more competitive Europe and a more agile Europe through smarter regulation.
Mr. President, the number of people without shelter, as well as those living below the poverty line, continues to rise in several Member States in recent years.
The economic, financial, and social crisis, along with rising unemployment, have exacerbated this problem, as everyone has already diagnosed.
We also all agree that this phenomenon constitutes a denial of important values of the European Union, respect for human dignity and respect for fundamental rights.

In my country, for example, which is subject to a stringent austerity policy, the concept of homelessness, according to the National Plan, encompasses people who go to seek assistance in order to have a meal.
The number of people seeking help for eating disorders has increased by more than 20% since 2008.
An increase is observed among families and individuals facing deprivation who have experienced a change in their living conditions due to unemployment and also due to bank debt.
This is just one example illustrating the seriousness of this situation.
Last year, the European Parliament adopted a written declaration on an EU strategy for the homeless.

With this statement, this Parliament has highlighted the need for the European Union to adopt a sustained strategy supported by national and regional strategies, with the long-term objective of ending the situations of people sleeping rough and long-term homeless individuals.
National strategies should focus on preventive measures, as well as concrete actions to improve the quality of services provided to the homeless and the availability of affordable housing.
It is also clear that this European strategy must be compatible with the social housing policies of the Member States.
We have carefully noted the Commissioner's remarks and have taken account of the situation he outlined.
I conclude by urging the European Commission to provide strong impetus to coordinate the actions of the Member States and regional authorities in combating this social injustice.
We have debated the peace process in the Middle East. But is there some ongoing process that I have missed?
Unfortunately, no.

The European Union repeatedly expresses, as does the majority of the international community, its support for the existence of two peoples and two states sharing the space between the Jordan River and the Mediterranean, each having the right to aspire to live in peace and security.
But without dialogue between the parties, caught in an escalation of hatreds and misunderstandings that has persisted for 63 years, only extremism and frustration are nurtured.

It is essential that Europe and the Arab League become much more actively involved in seeking a negotiated solution for the borders, based on those existing in 1967, with swaps that would allow the majority of Jewish settlements to remain within Israel.

That the issue of Jerusalem, the holy city, future capital of the two States, be clarified and mutually accepted as a symbol of peaceful coexistence between two peoples who bear the duty to fulfill the prophecy of Isaiah that this is the land of milk and honey.

That all countries of the Union participate in resolving the issue of Palestinian refugees, and that the international community, and in particular the Arab nation, participate in the necessary financial compensation.

It remains absolutely essential that a military force under the auspices of the United Nations, including participation by American and Islamic forces among others, be deployed on the ground as guarantor of peace maintenance.
Thus, let there be courage and political will.
Mr. President, Commissioner, tourism requires a clear and coordinated European approach, with careful attention to the potentials and challenges that the various tourism modes offer us.
The tourist diversity presented by the European Union is so rich that it enables exponential growth in this sector and enhances the competitiveness of the European Union itself.

A bet on this key sector, which accounts for 10% of GDP and approximately 12% of employment in the European Union, will not only create European added value but also help address the economic crisis we are facing.
It originates from an overseas region of Madeira, where tourism is the main economic activity.

Therefore, the creation of a specific heading for the sector in the 2014-2020 financial framework is essential for the cohesive and sustainable development of tourism, which is primarily composed of small and medium-sized enterprises.

The realization of a genuine European tourism policy, sustainable and diversified, requires a concrete strategic plan with a defined schedule, actions, and funding, capable of enhancing the visibility and quality of the tourism sector in the European Union.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. The absence of an ongoing peace process is undermining fundamental rights and has led to unilateral measures being taken by both sides, which lack legitimacy.

has contributed in no way to easing tensions in the region, and even less to the genuine pursuit of a life of peace, security, and dignity for the two peoples who have the right to live together harmoniously.

The time has come for the international community to say enough. The time has come for Europe and the Arab League to drive the search for a lasting solution, with reason, by presenting viable options without delay and within defined timeframes.
It is evident that the United States and Russia are no longer part of the solution.
Let us begin with Jerusalem. Why not revive the original idea of a separate corpus separatum administered by the United Nations, which would obviously serve as the capital of both states?
Borders. Let us begin with those from 1967.
Exchange land parcels whenever possible and consistent.
Take care of Israel's defense—an obviously unavoidable issue for the Jewish people. For instance, by deploying blue helmets alongside American, European, and Islamic forces along the Jordan.
Refugees, there are approximately 4.8 million of them, and they have the right to their dignity. To a future linked to a land, preferably their own.

But they are not exclusively an Israeli or Palestinian problem. They are also ours—those of the neighboring Arab countries, of the Arab community in general, as well as of Europe and the rest of the international community.
Colonies must end their expansion, and this is non-negotiable.
The announcement made today is shameful and reflects hypocrisy and bad faith in an announcement of readiness for immediate negotiations.

Above all, we want good-faith negotiations, committed to a deadline for their conclusion, from which point the international community, in order to maintain its credibility, will have to adopt a different stance.

Thank you, Madam President. Commissioner, the shameful situation in the refugee camps in Tunisia has already been mentioned here. We all share this concern, and I fully echo much of what my colleagues who have just spoken have said.
I will not refer to that particular aspect; I am referring to another situation that is even more overlooked—the citizens who were hosted in families' homes in Tunisia.

And this is one of the situations that should make us even more ashamed, because consider Tunisia, in a state of crisis, yet still capable of solidarity. Europe does not know how to be solidary and is not undergoing a transition toward democracy; it has not known how to be solidary, has not known how to receive.
I find it truly shameful that we can count the number of refugees received in Europe at only a few hundred.
Many of these people, contrary to what has been said, cannot return to their homes.
There were children who were enrolled in public schools, with implications for infrastructure and implications regarding the reception of these children in these countries.
That is precisely why, Madam Commissioner, I am deeply moved by your appeal, and I say this not ironically, but sincerely. But please, what about the Commission?
And in Europe,
What can you do about it?
How is it that Europe manages to live so well with this double standard?
We are so quick to repatriate and so slow to assist, Madam Commissioner.
It cannot continue like this.

Mr. President, I would naturally like to congratulate you on this additional hope you have given to all Members of the European Parliament and, in particular, to the citizens of the European Union whom we represent here.

And, having said that, I would like to emphasize that we now expect the Commission to take very firm action, in collaboration with the Council, so that the sovereign debt crisis can be resolved in the short term.
We know it's a marathon, but immediate signs are needed.

And therefore, without prejudice to our all beginning to consider an amendment to the treaties that would give political substance to the entire project we have brought forward here today, something needs to be done immediately.

this set of measures and perhaps an agreement, an agreement among states, what I have called a kind of parasocial agreement going beyond treaties, which could from this moment onward set all these ideas in motion.
Many congratulations on your courage and determination.
I believe we are on the right path and are leaving here today with renewed determination.
The crisis currently faced by the European Union has had serious consequences across all member states, and thousands of workers have been affected by layoffs caused by globalization and the crisis.
We cannot fail to express our solidarity with these workers who have lost their jobs, and to affirm that Europe must assist member states in maintaining employment rates and combating unemployment.
A swift reintegration of workers into the labor market is a priority objective.

An example of the usefulness of this fund is my country, Portugal, which has accessed it four times; however, we are aware that access to this fund could be improved, particularly in countries most affected by the crisis.

Thanks to the existence of the Globalization Adjustment Fund, it has been possible to assist thousands of workers through measures such as professional training, support for starting businesses, and incentives for recruitment.
This proposal is positive because it will enable Member States to request intervention from this Fund to support workers dismissed as a direct consequence of the economic and financial crisis.
We support extending the deadline and believe that the Member States most affected by the crisis should have greater and easier access to this fund supporting globalization and the crisis.
Thank you, Mr. President. I greet the Polish Presidency and also Commissioner Ali Reza, merely to say that I consider that...
Today, building upon and advancing what was accomplished in the State of the Union address, a very significant step was taken toward resolving the problems of the European zone crisis.
Because the Commission has here assumed its commitment to use its right of initiative and to work with the Council to resolve this crisis.
I believe this is an extremely important step, and it is the only way we have at this moment to resolve the eurozone crisis.
There is no possibility of resolving this in the short term through an intergovernmental approach alone. Therefore, it is necessary that the States, the governments of the States,
the commission the instruments and the mandate to lead this resolution process, to implement, execute, and develop the roadmap presented here.
But I did not want to fail to mention that this is not incompatible with our simultaneously developing, at the same time, our capacity to amend treaties.

I am also convinced that in the medium term it is necessary to amend the treaties, but even here we will have to do so in the direction of strengthening, of giving greater power and a stronger position to the Commission.

It is through reinforcing the community method—now in the short term, and subsequently through treaty changes in the long term—that we will achieve greater integration and a solution for the 17 countries of the eurozone and the 27 countries of the European Union.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. Dr. Durão Barroso came here today to announce that one of the measures requires banks that do not meet capital requirements to submit plans to achieve them, and if they fail to do so, they must then suspend dividends and bonuses.
I could not help but agree with the presentation made by the Mr. President.
This week we have seen what happened with Banco Zéxia, and just a few months ago, one of the bank's managers received a €600,000 bonus for his remarkably outstanding management, as we know.
But seriously, Mr. President, the Commission must leave the governments aside and present the complete package. And the complete package is what contains the essentials.
The essential point is that the money collected from contributors should be matched by a credit policy aimed at growth.
The essential point is that banks begin paying taxes on their profits, which does not occur in many countries, particularly in my country, Portugal.
In two years, rather, in three years, it is the second time we are being asked to rescue banks using money from taxpayers, from the unemployed.
Banks that save also the economy, helping to create jobs. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, High Representative, there are moments when I feel I need little and merely reflect, and this seems to be one of those moments.
It seems to me that this happens because we always move in step with events, responding to one event after another, and thus somewhat lose sight of the overall perspective.

It is true that each case is unique and each country we are discussing here is a specific case, but, pardon me for saying so, Ms. High Representative, it seems difficult to discern the criteria the European Union has applied in acting upon each of these cases.
We fear that we are often hostages to the interests of certain countries within the European Union, not of all of them.
And that is why we so often use in this House the expression "double standards," and we continue to use it.

Sometimes it is sanctions yes, sometimes sanctions no. Sometimes intervention goes as far as the bomb, as was the case in Libya. At other times it is total silence, as was the case during the invasion of Bahrain by the forces of

Saudi Arabia, and, Madam Baroness, let me tell you, I have carefully examined all the files, and I have not yet seen a single statement from European institutions condemning the invasion of Bahrain by the military forces of Saudi Arabia.

Death sentences are endless violence; they are doctors and nurses sentenced to up to 30 years in prison by a military tribunal simply for allegedly opposing the regime rather than aiding those injured who needed their help.
With regard to the dictatorial monarchy of Bahrain, to the dictatorial regime of Yemen, and to the regime of Saleh, we have had far more silence than action.

I am not in favor of interference, never have been. But I fear we may find ourselves in an uncomfortable position—seen from the outside, Mrs. Berenesa—which is, instead of helping development and aspirations,

democratic and revolutionary movements of the peoples in Arab countries, instead of helping to usher in the first Arab era, what we do is prevent its expansion—whether too much or too little—and I don't think we should bear that sin.
I hope the awarding of the Nobel Prize to an Uyghur activist serves as a signal that our side stands with the peoples in revolution. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Madam President. Commissioner, this is a good report by our colleague Casa.
First of all, one must ask why VAT should be revised in the European Union.
First of all, there is the need to align it with the single market
The more regimes and types of VAT there are, the less fluid the market will be.
A VAT reform benefits e-commerce, focuses competition on the intrinsic value and base price of goods and services, and not on fiscal differences.
Combats fraud and ensures the trust so essential, particularly in cross-border transactions. Enhances the economy of SMEs and reduces administrative burdens.
What other complementary measures are already underway in the single market that could be linked to the EIE?
First of all, a simple, clear, and transparent digital support mechanism, measure 7 of the single market.
Measure 5, on services, the need to review legislation on standardization, and the implementation of the Safety Directive.
Measure 9, aimed at removing fiscal barriers and explicitly reviewing the VAT system and VAT equalization in online and face-to-face trade, which we all experience when ordering books.
The pressures for VAT reform stem from two fundamental grounds.
First of all, the economic crisis.
Additional revenues are needed, including, in some countries, reducing payroll contributions to promote economic development by shifting this burden to VAT.

Secondly, the second major source of pressure is population aging, demographic aging, and the trend of shifting from indirect taxes to direct taxes, which is due to the reduction in the population base subject to taxation.
It is necessary to reconsider the balance between this direct and indirect taxation.
Finally, we must, in these reforms, reconcile subsidiarity with harmonization.
It is necessary to narrow the range of variation in the standard VAT rate, so that we can all understand it better.
Greater transparency is needed in defining the reduced VAT rate and its scope, along with a clear and widely disseminated list of reduced rates.
It is necessary to examine exemption thresholds for SMEs, bearing in mind that each exemption inevitably brings with it a series of minor or major frauds.
Finally, welcome the Commission's recent proposal to establish a European VAT.
Thank you, Madam President. I thank my colleague Rula for the excellent report she has prepared.

The advantages of public procurement are certainly well known, but the obstacles are equally evident. Conflicts of interest between European legislation and the objectives of the Member States undermine competitiveness.
The dichotomy, the quality of prices, and the complexity of laws and procedures are evident.
It is essential to examine existing best practices, particularly in Portugal, where electronic procurement has been functioning well and has facilitated public procurement.
An effective European public procurement represents a preferred means of controlling public deficit and fostering greater growth.
To achieve this, it should have a stable and predictable legal framework, less bureaucratic procedures to attract SMEs, and the protection of social and environmental values.

That is why I express my satisfaction with the intentions outlined by Commissioner Barnier here today regarding the review of public procurement, which will be one of the 12 priorities of the Single Market Act this year. Thank you.
I would like to congratulate my colleague Rula and all those who collaborated with her.
Important in the field of public contracts is the issue of public procurement and
that unfortunately the objectives proposed by the European Union—50% of case loads handled electronically by 2010—were not met, with actual figures reaching only the modest average of 5%.
Only a few countries, such as my own, Portugal, have succeeded in this area.

The advantages of electronic public procurement are too obvious to require further elaboration and have already been outlined here. It allows replacing costly access procedures with the simple consultation of the electronic platform, meaning it significantly reduces costs for companies, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises.

small and medium-sized enterprises, internet dissemination, and the ease of receiving procedural documents broaden the number of suppliers, increasing competition and enabling better purchases, thereby transforming the public procurement process

Easier and more flexible, it simplifies access to European Union public contracts, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises, thereby facilitating their participation in the public procurement market—key drivers of the economy.

In this regard, we welcome and encourage the European Commission for strengthening the mechanisms aimed at transforming public and electronic procurement into an increasingly comprehensive reality. Thank you very much.

We are living through decisive hours for Europe's fate. And although the situation has swiftly, before everyone's eyes, shifted from being seen as a problem of the Greeks or the Portuguese to a major European issue, the truth is that the inadequacy of the proposed solutions and their delay have produced no effective response to the rapid deterioration of the situation.
The search for solutions tailored to individual states rather than genuinely European solutions enabled markets to continue speculating on sovereign debts and imposed deadlines and rhythms.
absolutely impossible goals that some states are seeking to achieve through measures of a brutality previously unimaginable applied to individuals, businesses, and the public sector.

A common solution is absolutely imperative, as already found for the risk of European sovereign debt, which alleviates market pressure and speculation and provides time for the essential adjustment to be carried out.
Without this, at the end we will have countries that continue to lack the means to pay their creditors, destroyed economies, and much, much suffering.

To my fellow shadow rapporteurs, with whom I have had a fruitful collaboration, and also to the rapporteurs of the four involved committees' partners—I extend my thanks. These partners have made a valuable contribution to this report.
We received 400 amendments and developed 47 commitments, all of which were approved.
This demonstrates the importance and interest generated by this topic of new skills and jobs.
Ultimately, it is about preparing Europe's human resources, anticipating the needs of the labor market over the next decade.
The key strategy in preparing the report was to rephrase common ideas to avoid repetition and ensure the report's coherence.
I believe this objective has been achieved.
We all know that the crisis currently affecting us has highlighted the weaknesses of the European economic and social model, as well as reversed many of the advances achieved in the past.
Since the first effects of the crisis began to be felt in labor markets in 2008, Europe has already lost more than 5 million jobs.
Today, nearly 23 million people are unemployed.
Youth unemployment is even more alarming. The youth unemployment rate is close to 21% and represents more than double the overall unemployment rate.
On the other hand, in all Member States, unemployment rates vary according to qualification levels.
This means that people with higher qualifications find it easier to secure employment.
We also know that in the near future, demands for skills, competencies, and qualifications will increase across all types and levels of profession.
In this context, Europe faces four challenges. First, job creation. Second, the need to remain competitive.
Next, the sustainability of social security systems. Finally, the difficulty in finding individuals with the appropriate skills to fill job vacancies.

This report thus has a dual objective. First, to emphasize the need to act swiftly in order to close the skills gaps across Europe. Second objective, to better anticipate labor market needs regarding skills.

To this end, several measures stand out. First, to ensure the availability of qualified labor, it is necessary to improve basic skills, as well as competencies in science, innovation, information and communication technologies, and foreign languages.
As I have already mentioned, competency needs must be identified as early as possible, within a timeframe of at least 10 years.
Today, we recognize the potential of a new sustainable economy, the green economy, and the need for investment in ecological qualifications.
Subsequently, participation in lifelong learning and professional requalification should be increased, along with the reduction of school drop-out.
Another important issue is the mutual recognition of diplomas and professional qualifications, and the urgency of advancing this recognition.
Fourth, we draw attention to the connection that must exist between educational institutions and the needs of the labor market.
Internships must be of high quality and supervised by mentors, so as to lead to employment opportunities and eliminate any potential abuses arising from the internship status.
Particular emphasis is also placed on the role of social partners and social dialogue.
The report also proposes measures to promote demand and job creation.
We have a future ahead of us, and we must create jobs.
And small and medium-sized enterprises play a decisive role in achieving this objective.

It is also necessary to strengthen the single market to facilitate the free movement of workers, and thus the need to coordinate social security systems so that workers can have their acquired rights guaranteed, particularly the portability of pensions.

Lastly, the issue of labor market flexibility. To improve the functioning of the labor market, the rules of flexi-security must be adapted to the socioeconomic contexts of each Member State. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Madam President. I begin by thanking all colleagues who have participated in this lively debate.
All colleagues delivered very useful contributions and helped foster a highly constructive discussion on an ambitious agenda, at a time when we are experiencing a serious crisis.

Indeed, this debate is taking place precisely on the eve of a European Council that will show whether there is or is not political will to overcome the difficulties we are experiencing and whether there is will
policy for a deeperening of our economic coordination, and whether there is indeed a shared and unified will to achieve the ambition set out in this agenda. In other words, to have

new jobs in the coming decade, new jobs that give hope to young people who are currently unemployed and to those who are currently being trained, hope in an inclusive labor market that responds to their aspirations

the ambition of self-dignification through work, and also the opportunity to remain in a constant state of dynamism in order to keep pace with the challenges of a globalized economy, one whose pace has no parallel in recent generations.

Therefore, our ambition is genuinely to create jobs and achieve economic growth, not only to address the needs of those who are unemployed as a result of the crisis, but also of those who will enter the labor market.

responding to the major challenges ahead with a green economy, services in the health sector, and services in transportation and accommodation, all of which require from each of us a different, intelligent, and inclusive vision of the labor market.
Thank you very much to everyone.

Marisa Matias. Thank you very much, Madam President. What we were told today in this Council statement is that, for technical reasons, democracy would have to be sacrificed in the context of the European Semester. But what we are discussing here is a political issue. And the question I pose is whether political coordination can exist without democracy.
Where exactly is the scrutiny of the European Parliament?
Where is the scrutiny of National Parliaments over their major budgetary choices?

But if the issue were technical, as it has been stated here, then we are discussing a variable technique. Because we know how the Council substantially eased the conditions for the four countries on the waiting list—France, Italy, Hungary, and Spain—and did not do the same for the countries under rescue programmes.
Therefore, what kind of intergovernmentality is this that allows Germany to give opinions about Portugal, but does not allow Portugal to give opinions about Germany?
And, Mr. Spokesperson, I kindly ask you only to pay attention to this: no problem of democratic legitimacy is solved by asking deputies to consult information online.
This is an issue of information overload, not a problem of democratic exclusion—that is precisely the point we are discussing here. Thank you very much.

Thank you, Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues. A first word to congratulate Mr. Ardenne, the rapporteur, on the quality and timeliness of this report, as well as Commissioner Andor for the initiatives announced.
Poverty is indeed a very serious problem that requires effective and swift action to halt the growing phenomenon, which currently affects more than 80 million Europeans.
The severe crisis we are already experiencing has worsened this situation and made the times we live in dramatic, as its Commissioner Andor stated.
The number of people unemployed continues to rise, and we all know that unemployment is a gateway to poverty and social exclusion.
This situation requires resources, determination, and great effort from the European Commission and the Member States.
If this does not happen, the goal of lifting 20 million European citizens out of poverty during this decade will not be achieved.
Therefore, I warmly welcome the initiative of the Platform Against Poverty, which is a very important instrument for ensuring social justice.
But no one doubts that the best remedies against poverty and social exclusion are job creation and the promotion of economic growth.
Mr. President, access to the internet must today be regarded as a new fundamental right of citizens, and not merely as a commodity, because of this right
they depend on essential factors such as freedom of expression, access to and sharing of information, access to and sharing of culture and knowledge, as well as democratic and media pluralism.

And that is why this access cannot be subject to speculative pricing or contracts that obstruct free choice and the ease of switching providers, particularly in the field of mobile telecommunications, as we well know.
Active citizenship therefore requires that complete and transparent information be provided.
The blocking of internet access is not exclusive to political regimes that fear their people.

It may also happen that, as a result of economic considerations, options are offered to police customers, providing them with privileges, and this constitutes a less equitable treatment for customers who are not as profitable.
It is precisely for all these reasons that I support the Parliament in clearly positioning itself in favor of internet neutrality and the preservation of its universally inclusive nature.
Let us speak the truth.
We have been taking the medication for three years to get out of the crisis, and the situation is worsening day by day.
Instead of healing, we created two fatal vicious circles.
The first, between banks and states.
In Europe today, it is easier to discuss the failure of a state than that of a major bank, yet there remains an expectation that banks finance states and that states rescue their banks.
This spiral must be broken.
The Commission must advance European legislation that protects taxpayers from bank crises.
But it is also vital to protect the debt from attacks by speculators.

And for this, it is urgent and already by the afternoon to prepare coordinated emissions of sovereign debt—whether called Eurobonds or Stability Bonds—and guarantee them through robust, community-based instruments of a non-intergovernmental nature.
In short, we also need to reconsider the involvement of the European Central Bank.
But the second vicious circle arises from the argument that some states are rich because they are virtuous, while others are poor because they have sinned.
This narrative is politically explosive and technically incorrect.

She ignores the essential fact that a single market and a single currency, built through political choice among unequal partners, necessarily exacerbates competitiveness imbalances between the center and the periphery.
And this is what must be anchored in economic governance.
No, Mr. Rompói, through sanctions that promote the suppression of wages and incomes by one country exerting control over another.
before, through engagement, Mr. Commissioner, both with countries that have surpluses and those that have deficits, in order to converge productivity through investment, education, and science.
Sanctions have only generated unemployment, disillusionment, and a wave of hardship from which we cannot escape.

Thank you very much, Madam President. Madam Commissioner, the integrated maritime policy advocates a coordinated approach to the management and governance of oceans, seas, and coasts, within a global and coherent framework of all European policies related to the sea.

Having a global perspective and an integrated approach implies the interaction of European policies related to the sea and demands improved governance through greater involvement in the European decision-making process.
The success and relevance of this policy have been demonstrated over recent years through the various initiatives funded in the form of preparatory actions and pilot projects.
Integrated maritime policy must therefore continue to benefit from adequate funding in the coming years of the current financial programming period.

In this regard, I advocate for appropriate funding up to 2013, not only to consolidate previous projects, but also support the establishment of a stable framework regarding the objectives of this programme.

Issues concerning policy, governance, sustainability, and maritime surveillance are, for obvious reasons, particularly relevant to certain regions of the European Union, such as the outermost regions.

The eruption regions, with the exception of French Guiana, are small in size and rely on a limited number of economic activities, among which maritime and fishing activities assume particular importance.
Moreover, the outermost regions contribute to making the European Union possess the world's largest exclusive economic zone.

The SPAs represent more than half of the European Union's exclusive economic zone, approximately 15 out of 25 million square kilometers, and, together with coastal regions, are essential for the economic growth of the European Union and its member states.

A program such as the one outlined in the Integrated Maritime Policy should benefit these regions by prioritizing transnational coordination of maritime policies and relevant international cooperation.
It is therefore necessary to ensure continuous and sufficient funding, not only for the next two years, but also and above all for the upcoming financial framework starting in 2014.
Failing to seize the opportunity when discussing integrated maritime policy to highlight the immense potential that the Atlantic and insular dimensions hold for the European Union.

The Atlantic, as Europe's western maritime frontier, also establishes its connection to North and South America and to Africa, imparting an essential strategic dimension for Europe within the framework of a maritime policy designed with a forward-looking perspective.

And it is with the future in mind that the integrated maritime policy, beyond promoting the coordination of maritime activities, must place the potential of the Atlantic in its three-dimensional dimension—based on science and technology—at the top of its priorities.

The exploration of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, the deep sea, and the seafloor represents a new frontier whose potential in biotechnology and mineral resources is incalculable in value to the knowledge-based economy.
The Union should therefore take advantage of the Azores' privileged position and the excellence of its oceanographic department to establish a deep-sea observatory there. Thank you.
Enough of this path.
When the diagnosis is wrong, the medicines don't work.
And in the case of the eurozone, excessive remedies have brought it close to death.
The successive miracle solutions that were proposed were likewise discredited by the markets.
the Financial Stabilization Fund, the successive tightening measures of the Stability and Growth Pact, and the revenues from the Troika in low-program countries.
Blind discipline without growth triggered a recessive wave across fully interconnected economies, which thus became contaminated by bankruptcies and unemployment.
And in the hands of the markets—that is, of rating agencies and speculators—governments are one by one collapsing, and solvent countries are becoming insolvent.

Amending the treaties now is another step along this disastrous path, with an added drawback: we risk destroying what remains of national sovereignty, placing the countries in the hands of the European Court of Justice.
No, it is not necessary to amend the treaties.
We must, in this emergency we are facing, make two decisions.
First of all, a genuine agenda for sustained growth and cohesion must be implemented.
Where is the 2020 Agenda?
Where is the relaunch budget?
Where are the so-called project bonds for Europe's structural investments?
And secondly, sovereign debt must be protected.
Of course, the bailouts are necessary, but in this emergency we are currently facing, it is urgent that the European Central Bank, directly or indirectly, defend sovereign debt against speculators.
Not in a shamefaced manner, but with a firm, resolute stance, as befits any central bank defending its currency and the economy that supports it.
For this, it is not necessary to amend the treaties; what is needed is political will and European conviction.

Let us revise the treaty once we have drawn all the lessons from the current crisis. And there are certainly many lessons to be learned, but this is certainly not the right moment. Thank you very much.
Mr. President and Commissioner Dalli, the first Single Market Forum, an initiative stemming from the Greek report, took place in Kraków.
It was rich in content and fruitful in the debate conducted by the Polish presidency with European, national, regional, and local authorities and civil society.
We have a roadmap outlined in the Commission's communication of 12 April, with its 12 levers
It needs to be enacted into law, as well as the strategy for the other 38 priorities identified in the Single Market Act.
For a market to exist, trust is necessary.
Now, citizens' and consumers' trust cannot be taken for granted; it must be stimulated and nurtured.

The single market is not merely a buying and selling operation. It involves horizontal policies, such as health, social protection and consumer rights, labor law, the environment, and sustainable development.
The Single Market Forum should therefore become an emblematic event for the European Union, held annually and attracting a diverse and increasingly participatory audience.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I believe we are at a moment of assessment regarding these crisis policies, austerity policies that have affected so many countries, as well as the so-called structural reforms.
And it is within this context that I believe we cannot fail to recognize how much social commotion they have generated.
And it has generated social unrest not only in countries facing greater difficulties, in southern countries, not only in so-called peaks, but also in other contexts across Europe.
Last week we were in Portugal during the largest general strike ever, both in the public and private sectors. A strike that took to the streets.

But if we look at what has happened today in the United Kingdom, we see that we have witnessed the largest strike ever, especially when we consider that people took to the streets in the same way they did in Portugal.
And we expect a large demonstration in Brussels on Friday.

What this demonstrates, Mr. President, as do strikes in the public demonstrations, is that the causes and problems we have been facing are common, and it is precisely because of this that the response is also common.
The crisis of the European Union is affecting all member states, reaching incredible proportions.

We have witnessed chaos in financial markets and the growing inability to secure the funding essential for the operations of our small and medium-sized enterprises—enterprises that are otherwise healthy—driving them to despair, bankruptcy, and unemployment.
It is a spiral that fuels recession and is destroying the economy and society.

No one denies the need for strict budgetary discipline, deep restructuring, and stronger economic governance. But these are necessary conditions that, in the short term, are no longer sufficient to reverse the ongoing deterioration within the Union.

And if the truth is that we need effective policies to stimulate employment and growth, without which we will never succeed in controlling sovereign debts, the central issue today is ensuring the financing of our businesses and our economies, without which the Union will plunge into crisis.
Therefore, more than ever, more than treaty revisions, we today need a strong and decisive intervention by the European Central Bank.
Thank you very much, Madam President, Commissioner. We are all here today united around a resolution containing a set of very important demands in terms of intervention.
We are referring to World AIDS Day, and this review of the 2009 resolution and communication is essential.
It is essential, precisely, things that have already been mentioned
We repeat ourselves because, unfortunately, there is still room for repetition, which means there is much yet to be done.
There are many new cases diagnosed every year in Europe and the European Economic Area. A 2010 report mentions more than 25,000 new cases.
If we consider the neighboring countries, we are talking about more than 100,000 new cases.
And if we think there are things that cannot get worse, they can always get worse.
And one of the issues I wish to make very clear here concerns the disinvestment that has occurred in the health sector as a result of the current economic and financial crisis we are experiencing.
And this disinvestment has seriously affected areas that are fundamental to the fight against HIV/AIDS.
That is precisely why, Mr. Commissioner, I believe the Commission has a crucial role to play in urging the Member States to fulfill their obligations in these areas.

A second issue also relates to the increase in discrimination, evidenced by the economic and social crisis. Discrimination against workers who have lost their jobs due to being carriers of this virus.
And this is why prevention, treatment, and care must be equal for everyone, regardless of sex, age, or the region in which they live.
I hope we succeed in implementing these measures with the support of the Commissioner.
Welcome to this Parliament, Mr. Draghi.
We are living through special and dramatic circumstances, and they require determined and urgent measures.

The spiral between government debt and bank debt must be broken, but at this moment the priority is defending the states, ensuring that eurozone countries and the euro itself are not destroyed by rating agencies and speculators.
In the report we will approve, Tremosa and Bacel present proposals to rethink the architecture of the eurozone, create eurobonds, and establish a European treasury. But all of this takes time.
At this moment we are discussing an emergency, and within this context, the only solution lies in the hands of the European Central Bank.
Technically, the European Central Bank knows what needs to be done.
Today you should not be inhibited in your intervention and action by narrow interpretations of your mandate.
Its mandate is to safeguard the euro, not to address inflation.
At a moment like this, there is no doubt. Saving the euro and doing whatever is necessary is precisely what this Parliament and the citizens expect from the European Central Bank.
Good luck and courage, Mr. Tragui.
The European Neighbourhood Policy already explicitly included the positions that the European Parliament has been adopting.

And with great satisfaction, we saw that a significant portion of the principles and proposals we have been advocating in this House were included in the subsequent communications from the Commission and the Council.

I would like to express my gratitude, moreover, for the excellent and ongoing collaboration and coordination we have maintained throughout this period with Vice-President Catherine Ashton and Commissioner Stefan Füle and their respective offices.
right from the start through the improvement achieved in our relationship with neighboring peoples.

By prioritizing relations with the general population and, in particular, with civil society organizations, we will be lending greater objectivity and greater effectiveness to our neighborhood policy, which is intended to be founded exclusively on the universal values we uphold and share.
I emphasize, however, that the European Union does not intend to impose a predefined model or formula for political reforms in third countries.
But when it comes to the money of our taxpayers, we should give more to those who are doing more to advance the values we believe in.
This principle means that the faster a country progresses in its internal reforms, the more support it will receive from the European Union.
The principle of more for more is therefore correct, fair, and consistent.
This obviously means that we also anticipate the possibility of cases or countries where we will have less for less.
The neighborhood policy is now based on the promotion of shared values and a commitment to democracy, human rights, the rule of law, and good governance.
But this pursuit implies greater accountability.
Our partners are called upon to assume greater responsibility in the process. That is why we advocate the principles of greater differentiation, greater conditionality, and more for more.

By adopting an approach of engagement with our partners from the bottom up, and implementing customized policies—what we refer to as the bottom-up approach and tailor-made policies—we enhance the effectiveness of development and institutional capacity.

The institutional framework that we so strongly wish to defend and that lies at the foundation of the Rule of Law and the democratic process, which we wish to see implemented as quickly as possible in the societies, economies, and communities of our neighboring countries.

The idea is to give a voice to citizens, as well as to all civil society movements that identify with our values, by supporting organized movements that strive for positive and democratic changes in their respective countries.

Thank you very much, Madam President. The Commission and the Governments now pledge to reprogram structural funds to support SMEs and create jobs.

and stimulating employment, particularly youth employment, could not be more welcome, especially for economies undergoing stringent austerity measures, such as the Portuguese economy.

Mr. President, Commissioner, the milk package presented here as a tool for sector organization contains several positive and significant elements, such as strengthening the role of producers' organizations and interprofessional organizations, as well as contractual aspects that in certain Member States may, together with workers, cease to apply.

Yet, far short of the instruments the sector needs to thrive in the coming years, within a vision of agriculture we wish to see in Europe—ensuring fair distribution of value throughout the food chain and preventing agriculture from becoming dominated by large-scale intensive operations concentrated in just a few Member States.

If improving the self-regulation condition in the relationship among sector actors is desirable, merely assuming that this alone is sufficient constitutes a serious error that could exclude thousands of milk producers, dozens of regions, and even some dairy-producing countries from the sector.

The debate on the regulation of the dairy sector from the perspective of an economically, socially, environmentally, and territorially sustainable sector remains ongoing and is still to be fully realized.
And we cannot abandon the milk quota system without having ensured genuine instruments for managing supply.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner. From May 2010 to the present, we have witnessed how a problem that initially appeared manageable has worsened in an uncontrolled manner.
It is true that the fight against the crisis triggered by financial markets worsened the debt levels of countries in the eurozone, and that the most vulnerable ones became especially exposed.

However, is it possible that countries sharing the same currency should be left to the whims of financial operators and those profiting from bets on the likelihood of eurozone countries going bankrupt?
The severe consequences of the Council's and the Commission's inability to respond have generated problems that are today undeniable.
Speculative interest rates applied to individual countries have made any strategy for exiting the crisis unviable.

The resulting worsening of the country's economic problem justifies a renewed increase in interest rates, until the complete withdrawal of credit kills the country's economy and contagion takes care of the rest.
This Parliament has long been a form of common management of sovereign debt, subject to strict but clear rules.
We welcome the fact that the Commission has finally produced a green paper on the subject, referred to as Brazil.

It is now clear that without some form of such commitments, properly linked to the European stabilization mechanism, the so-called protection barriers, the so-called firewalls, for sovereign debt do not exist. And as long as they do not exist, the euro is at risk, and Europe as well.

In the resolution adopted by this Parliament by an overwhelming majority regarding the new treaty, it became absolutely clear that it is unacceptable to continue imposing further fiscal stability rules, budgetary discipline, and sanctions on euro area countries without taking concrete and urgent steps toward the joint management of sovereign debt.
The two processes will have to progress in parallel.
That is what we expect from the Commission, and it is already too late.

I would like to believe that this House of European Democracy will unanimously condemn the conduct of President Assad and the Syrian authorities in the massacre of their own people, which we helplessly witness on a daily basis.

Because it is important that Europe be able to speak with one voice regarding the meaning of the values of respect for human life, even in the face of a massacre that is indiscriminate and nobly orchestrated.
The entire international community must maintain pressure on the Syrian regime through the imposition of new restrictive measures.
The killings must stop, and this is non-negotiable.
Insist on dialogue, undoubtedly.

A democratic Syria requires a transitional period and guarantees for all its minorities. However, the implementation of more stringent measures against the Syrian regime does not ensure that all countries will row in the same direction. And this clearly and unfortunately has not happened.
In addition to thousands of dead and the two revolutions shamefully rejected by the United Nations Security Council,
We continue to condemn the position of China and Russia, increasingly isolated in their incredible support for the Syrian regime.
Everything else remains unchanged. A rotten, desperate, and cornered regime continues to govern a country. And we, the international community, remain powerless to stop the genocide of a people.
We are already behind schedule in strengthening the action of the European Union and the international community.
We must provide more concrete support to the Arab League in its interventions and its intention to deploy a peacekeeping force to Syria.
We must fully support the Red Crescent's work in its efforts to minimize the suffering of populations.
The International Criminal Court must immediately initiate proceedings aimed at punishing those responsible for crimes being committed against humanity.

Mr. President, I would essentially like to say that from the upcoming Council, we expect a major economic decision for countries under aid closely linked to the issue of structural funds.

Thank you, Mr. President, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner. I greet the rapporteurs, but I would like to say: enough with pious speeches about employment and growth.

Today, with the first results on macroeconomic imbalances in Europe, the veil begins to lift on why, within the European Union, and particularly in the eurozone, some countries grow while others inevitably unravel.

Europe is losing competitiveness in external markets, and this agenda must be reassessed, as it is through this channel that much of the insufficient domestic growth is routed. But, focused on the domestic market,
Four countries, including Germany, consistently accumulate surpluses year after year, while 20 other countries, I repeat, 20, also consistently accumulate deficits year after year.
As deficits accumulate, the external debt of these countries increases in an unsustainable manner.
Of course, this process must be controlled.
Of course discipline is needed.
But today it is important to ask ourselves: in this context, is crushing the more vulnerable with recessive policies really the only possible solution?
Will the fiscal competition among certain winners within the internal market continue to be tolerated?

Can the largest European economy continue to pursue common policies that favor it, along with internal economic policies that, while appropriate for its own economy, capture most of the benefits of the internal market for itself?
Can the Commission and the Council continue to impose recessive policies that tighten the economy of the most vulnerable, while discussing
openly the possibility of sovereign eurozone states going bankrupt, when they know that firewalls never left the realm of intentions?
Do the Commission and the Council fully realize the explosive political-economic risk that this speech poses to Europe?
Yes, recipes need to be revised in a radical way.

While this will does not exist, out of respect for citizens, let us cease claiming that we support growth and employment, because it is not true.
And citizens cannot continue to be deceived.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. I begin by welcoming the rapporteurs.

Saying that we are living through the most severe financial, economic, and social crisis since the establishment of our modern international financial model has already become a commonplace statement.

Measures to ensure that young people will not remain without a job or training offer for more than four months, as well as to reduce the risk of poverty and social exclusion, must become a reality.

But unfortunately, this does not seem to be the truth.
A year after Fukushima, new nuclear power plants continue to be built.
A year after Fukushima and new nuclear power plants continue to be planned.
Japan unfortunately carries the memory of enduring tragedies—Hiroshima, Nagasaki—and we Europeans likewise bear the memory of a lasting tragedy, Chernobyl.
Fukushima compels us to reopen a debate that should never have been closed—the debate on nuclear energy.
A year ago, nuclear energy was unquestionable for the Japanese. Today, two-thirds of the Japanese do not want it.
They know it costs lives; they know this cost is immeasurable. On the contrary, it is a cost that grows from generation to generation.
The policy stubbornly in favor of nuclear energy is the policy of arrogance.
Here in Europe, we also have a majority of public opinion opposed to nuclear energy, and yet nothing seems to be changing.
There are currently 320,000 refugees in Japan. Of these, 100,000 will never be able to return home.
What was once familiar is now dangerous. What was once comfort is now ruin.
It is the ruin of a building or a house with radioactive vegetation. It is the ruin of soil or water contaminated with radiation.
Fukushima cannot be visited like other ruins, for example a Mayan ruin or a Roman ruin. Yet, like these, Fukushima also portrays a civilization. And the ruin of Fukushima portrays our civilization.
the one that chose to include a deadly technology in its development model.
