As already mentioned, diabetes affects more than 30 million people in Europe.
These are indeed figures from an epidemic.
And that is why I believe it is also justified to adopt a specific resolution on diabetes, especially since this is the first time this Parliament is addressing this issue.
It is the result of collective effort and represents a work that does not negate the work previously done on chronic diseases; on the contrary, it strengthens our capacity to act together.

We have approximately 325,000 deaths annually associated with diabetes, as we know that diabetes is the leading cause of, for example, heart attacks, blindness, amputations, and failure of vital organs.
And given these data, we have two options, in my view.
The first is to say that it isn't that serious, that we can live well with this, and that each government should take care of itself.
The second point is to say that the absence of a European strategy in this area is part of the problem.
I understand that on this side we have a great deal to accomplish.
and we have much to accomplish in early diagnosis, coordination, and investment in research.
And that is why this resolution has the value of a beginning and of joint work in this direction. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Mr. President. What I would like to highlight, essentially, is to congratulate you on the outcomes of the Council.
Not because the measures are ideal, but because they are the feasible measures.
There is no doubt that today we have come much further and can be more optimistic than we were three months ago and six months ago.

Many states have made enormous efforts toward the stabilization and consolidation of their public finances, and this effort has only been undertaken by some states due to the inducement of the European Union.
It is essential for them to regain their credibility in international markets, and it is essential for them to grow in the long term.
With these guarantees that states have provided, among which the Portuguese state stands out, regarding fiscal consolidation and budgetary consolidation, I am
convinced, also with the new treaty signed by the 25, that we will be in a position to genuinely relaunch growth in the upcoming steps of the European Council.
But only with this confidence, which is gradually being generated, can that growth be achieved.
And therefore I say, solidarity from the wealthier with the poorer is important, but solidarity from the poorer with the wealthier is also important, providing these guarantees.
Mr. President, I would like to say, essentially, that we are genuinely pleased to see that steps have been taken towards integrating Serbia into the European Union.

This is very important because the experience of countries such as Portugal, Spain, or Greece—nations that lived under dictatorships and faced a series of very serious problems—demonstrates that European integration was the means by which democracy was consolidated.
And for this reason, Serbia also deserves this opportunity.
In any case, I would like to offer here a note somewhat different from the one provided by my colleagues.

I am aware of and welcome the great concern for minorities, but it is essential not to forget the Serbian majority. It is crucial that Serbs themselves have a place in the European Union and a place of full rights.
This must not be forgotten.

There will be no peace in the Balkans without Serbia. And therefore, however much we emphasize the rights of minorities—and they must be respected—we must not forget the Serbian majority, their identity, and their full right to be European citizens, equal to all others, with their own state and their own national pride.
Thank you, Mr. President. Portugal voted last week on the budgetary ultimatum, which in fact is not a European treaty, as it should be noted.

The governing parties' favorable vote reflects decades upon decades of stupidity and the typical attitude of a good student, sycophantic and obedient, who does not think and blindly follows bad teachers.
The position of the PS and its leader was to move from a dangerously violent abstention to a crawling vote in favor.

This alliance makes our Assembly of the Republic, the Portuguese one, the first parliament to accept the humiliation of depriving itself of one of its most important functions for which the people elected it: to decide annually the country's budget.
This is yet another blow from the European Union, yet another. It is a blow in which the power of money becomes increasingly beyond the reach of citizens' votes.
And I ask you, Mr. President, is it democratic for someone to vote in such a way that they can no longer vote? Thank you.
Budgetary consolidation is imposing almost unbearable demands on some European citizens.
However, each day fewer people are those who have to bear a greater burden.
The tax burden falls on workers, small and medium-sized enterprises, and consumers, while at the same time witnessing completely disproportionate tax evasion and tax flight.
The European socialists group wanted to know how much this tax evasion and tax fraud amounts to across the 27 countries. And they amount to one trillion euros.
This is more than the countries of the European Union spend on health.

This means a volume such that if we spent only one-fifth of this amount on investment, investment in Europe would rise from 2.7% of GDP to 3.5%. We are therefore discussing an exceptionally high value.

And the meaning of the resolution we are about to adopt, initiated by this Parliament, is precisely a very clear demand on the Commission and the Council to accelerate concrete action against tax evasion and fraud.

This means, in particular, concrete initiatives for tax cooperation within the European Union, as well as tax cooperation with our neighboring countries, in particular Switzerland, but negotiated by the Commission and not on a country-by-country bilateral basis.
This means reforming and harmonizing accounting rules, increasing transparency and reporting.
This clearly means reactivating the fundamental issue of combating tax fraud and tax evasion through tax havens.
Europe is not a lesser partner.
Europe is a major partner in the international context
Europe has the obligation, within the framework of the G20, to clearly advocate a joint fight against fraud and tax evasion.

Without this, we lose credibility with our citizens, and therefore, my final words in this debate are a call for the Commission and the Council to launch an effective fight against fraud and tax evasion. Thank you very much.

Thank you, Mr. President. I begin by congratulating my colleague, Ms. Nibler, on her excellent work, and by noting the coincidence that today, on Europe Day, we are debating and tomorrow will approve measures that respond to the legitimate aspirations of our citizens.

What is at stake in this regulation is imposing limits on mobile phone tariffs charged to European consumers when roaming, with its main objective being to end roaming charges within the European space starting from 2016.
Our citizens will now benefit from a significant reduction in prices regarding calls, SMS messages, and data.
Today, there are operators charging €3 per megabyte, but from July onwards, the maximum price they can charge will be 70 cents per megabyte, decreasing to 20 cents per megabyte in 2014.
Regarding incoming calls, the maximum price that may be charged will be 8 cents this year and 5 cents in 2014.
In July 2012, a family with two mobile phone users will save, as the Honourable Commissioner said, 200 euros on their bill.
Second benefit: higher quality of information provided to consumers.
Starting from summer, operators will be required to inform consumers of the prices charged when they travel outside the European Union as well.
Until now, the obligation applied only within the European space.
Third advantage: the possibility of more operators entering the market, which means increased competition that will encourage better services and lower prices.

Finally, in the near future, the consumer may, if desired, have two contracts—one for services within the national territory and another for services abroad—while keeping the same mobile number and receiving only one bill.
The consumer, our citizen, will likewise be granted the possibility of contracting with any European operator offering them the best price.
This report is the perfect example of the Europe of citizens, the Europe of freedom of movement and freedom of establishment. Many thanks.
Mr. President, in February 2009 this Parliament adopted a report supporting the European Commission's proposal to revive the European economy.
There were projects on clean energy, transportation, new technologies, and innovation, and even at that stage the role of the European Investment Bank and structural funds was being considered.
After more than three years, we managed to deliver the Six-Pack, the Europlus-Pack, the Fiscal Compact, and the Two-Pack, all of them featuring enhanced discipline and sanctions.
Yet meanwhile, as unemployment—particularly among youth—reaches alarming levels, the European economy enters recession, and more than seven of its member countries are experiencing negative growth,
And yet, while Greece is no more than a first stone threatening to bring down the castle, the revival agenda seems to have been forgotten.
At this moment, this agenda is clear.
What matters at this moment is no longer assigning it a sense of urgency, but rather a sense of emergency in a situation that threatens to explode.
Urgent intervention is needed in a set of key pillars.
First of all, it is essential that this European Marshall Plan be established through investment that promotes growth, employment, and convergence.
Project bonds are one of the possible sources.
Secondly, it is necessary to stabilize the interest rates at which countries finance themselves.
Let us at least prepare to coordinate sovereign debt issuance and jointly manage the debt that has already accumulated.
Third point: the European Stability Mechanism and the European Central Bank must prepare to actually function as genuine firewalls in defending European sovereign debt.
Fourth, the credit that was foolishly transferred by the European Central Bank to commercial banking must reach businesses.
It is a billion euros held up within the banking system while thousands of viable companies die every day due to lack of funding.
Fifth point: Investment makes no sense without demand.
The most vulnerable economies are crushed by austerity plans.
It is the strongest economies that must unleash domestic demand.

It is the case that nearly 80% of exchanges today take place within Europe. And it is the Commission's responsibility, Mr. Commissioner Olliren, to make recommendations directed not only at the most fragile economies, but also at the most dynamic ones.
And finally, Mr. President, we must set a limit on the revenues imposed by the tolls.

We must say enough to the punitive style, devoid of dialogue and accountability, by which senior officials determine the fate of millions of European citizens and the democratic institutions of member states. Many thanks, Mr. President.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Commissioners. What I would like to say is very clear. Naturally, the Portuguese members of the PPE fully support all measures that may contribute to growth. There is no possibility whatsoever of

if it exits this crisis without promoting growth, particularly in those states most affected by the financial crisis. But sometimes, when I hear some of my colleagues here, I get the impression that they believe growth can continue despite excessive deficits and poor public finances.

Now, we must continue to insist on spending reductions and structural reforms. We can do everything possible on the growth side, but we cannot abandon the path of controlling and reducing public expenditure and...

of structural reforms. More growth cannot mean more relaxation, more rest. More growth and more growth-oriented policies must go hand in hand with great rigor in public finances and with great rigor in structural reforms.
If we begin speaking only of growth and come to believe in magical solutions to growth, we will once again end up with an unsustainable public debt and continue without structural reforms.

That said, I would like to draw attention to the fact that, at this morning's summit, the issue at stake will not be solely this long-term or medium-term question of growth. The urgent matter of Greece will also be on the agenda. And in this regard, I believe it is essential that European states, in some way, stand alongside Greece.

We are responsible for finding a solution to the Greek problem. And I would like to reject here the statement made by the British Member of Parliament referring to the "Portugueseification" of Europe.
the Portuguese influence on Europe, at least on the level of values, were not a negative thing. And therefore, it rejects the idea of ultimately humiliating or seeking to diminish any peoples.
We are all Europe, and each of us has a place within it.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. Commissioner, you came here to tell us that every extra euro spent on debt is a euro less for job creation. Then explain to us why we should continue with this obsession.

He also came here to tell us that one of the summit's priorities is keeping Greece in the eurozone. And I ask you, Commissioner, do we believe you, or do we believe Dr. Durão Barroso or Mr. Trichet, who not so many days ago were saying exactly the opposite?
He spoke of maintaining the eurozone; I ask, which euro?

in the euro we currently live in, in this zone we currently inhabit, or in the euro announced yesterday by Deutsche Bank in a study referring to a G-euro, a second-tier euro, should the left win the elections in Greece in June.
This is a trap, Mr. Commissioner; it is the trap of a Europe held hostage by governments, and the trap of a Europe held hostage by financial interests.
Let us not allow people to live in fear, for it is not a way to live. Europe could be something else. You know, there is no lack of money, but a lack of shame.

Mr. President, Minister Vaman, the first thing I would like to state is that it is regrettable that the Vice-President of the Commission and the High Representative, Ms. Ashton, are not present at this important debate.
In any case, we are pleased to work once again with Minister Vaman, who here made a statement with which I fully identify.

First of all, I would like to offer a word of comfort and solidarity to the people of Guinea, namely the people of Guinea-Bissau, a people systematically massacred by democratic instability, political instability, and widespread insecurity.
Not only is he surrounded by poverty, but he has also been systematically affected by these afflictions.

I would like to say, on the other hand, that the PPE, during its presidency, has been following this issue from the outset. And that it is regrettable that the European Parliament only managed to hold a debate on this matter after a month.
This relates to the invisibility of the Guinean people and the Guinean issue.

And this invisibility stems from the fact that, unlike other equally serious issues, no major economic interests are at stake here—interests that would prompt European, American, and global powers to debate the situation of the Guinean people.
But I must say, if an external reason is needed, if poverty and political instability alone are not sufficient for us to take global humanitarian and political action, there is one.
unless the situation regarding drug trafficking escalating in Guinea were a reason for us to engage in this matter.
And therefore, I ask the European Union to be stronger here.

There is no doubt that Resolution 2048, there is no doubt that the decisions of the European Union, the community of Portuguese-speaking countries, and the African Union, but perhaps not anymore, the community of states of West Africa, are moving in the right direction.
But resolutions are not enough; actions are required.
We must be firmer, we must be more effective with the states that have the capacity to intervene in Guinea-Bissau.
That is our obligation.

And I believe that the European Union is currently responding appropriately in terms of principles, but is not responding as it should in terms of practical operational instruments on the ground. And it is this that I request of the Council and I request of the Commission.

Mr. President, Mr. President-in-Office, Commissioner, dear colleagues, globalization has brought about an disproportionate growth of the financial sector, whose transactions today, as is well known, are 70 times greater than global wealth.

Opacity, extremely frequent transactions, high remuneration, and excessive risk have not only characterized the world we live in today, but have also been fundamental causes of the crisis we are experiencing.
However, this crisis has demanded accountability and far greater efforts from ordinary citizens than from the sector that caused it.
Thus, today, this moment is important.

It is important, and I would personally welcome a situation in which the tax is generalized across all countries of the European Union and subsequently extended worldwide, through the active participation of the Commission and the European Union as a whole within the G20 context.
I would like it to encompass, or cover, all products without exception,
I would like it to serve as a basis for the necessary strengthening of the European Union's own resources.
In the short term, compromises must be accepted, but we must not lose sight of the direction in which we must move.
And this can only be achieved through a clear identification of these objectives by this Parliament, by the Commission, and with a strong spirit of commitment also from the Council.

This is what European citizens expect from us today, and I can only thank here the role not only of Anipolimata, which was tireless throughout this process, but also of all those who managed to overcome their differences to reach a common position.

Thank you, Mr. President, thank you, Ms. Podimata. Today, we are discussing here for the first time the tax on financial transactions, and its approval would be the first indication that speculation will not go entirely unpunished in the crisis we are currently experiencing.
This House and the European institutions must be accountable to people, not to financial markets.
For decades, financial markets' lobbies managed to govern the world without opposition and without any problems.
It is obvious to us that during 2008 and 2012 the crisis in the speculative financial market lay at the root of most of the problems we experienced.

And yet, these speculative financial markets managed not only to ensure that nothing was done, but also to effect a direct transfer from the pockets of citizens straight into these very speculative markets.

We have been fighting for this for many years, Mr. President, but it was only when peoples across much of Europe began to say enough that the institutions governing the European Union finally took some initiative.
And therefore, this is the answer we must give to citizens. It is a first signal we must give them.
The citizens of Europe, who have paid for everything, deserve not to be disrespected.
And therefore, this proposal is not complete, not perfect, and will not solve the entire problem of financial speculation, but it is an important signal.
We have been, no, European leaders have been apologizing to financial markets
They should apologize to the people they have affected.
And, to conclude, Mr. President, quoting a politician who is now famous: it is of these markets that we are speaking, of these markets that govern us without ever having been elected.

I sincerely hope that governments send a signal of confidence to people, and I sincerely hope that with this approval and the positive votes of my colleagues, we have begun to speak of justice and solidarity in Europe.
Colleagues, muster your courage—we'll push this project through.
Thank you, Mr. President. The financial transaction tax should serve as a means of controlling and discouraging high-risk speculative activities in the financial sector.
It aims to reduce speculation and foster healthy competition, the foundation of the single market, and also generate revenue.
The Podimata report sought to bring the European Commission's initial proposal closer to the well-known UK stamp duty. In this way, we made the proposal more attractive to hesitant member states.
It is expected that the Council will also understand the message.

Whereas in the Commission's proposal the tax applied to transactions conducted by financial institutions located within the territory of the European Union, it is now intended that transactions involving European assets, even when carried out by non-European entities, should also be subject to taxation.
This measure will broaden the tax base and reduce revenue losses.
It is expected that tax revenues will be regarded as own resources of the Union or of the Member States, thereby reducing the Member States' taxes—a matter still to be decided.
I conclude, Mr. President, by congratulating the rapporteur on the quality of her work and I hope that this symbolic text...
Madam President, we had yet another request this weekend to rescue the private banking sector.

They keep repeating the same argument, which is to prevent contagion to the eurozone. And I ask myself whether we have learned nothing. Greece, Portugal, Ireland, and now Spain—and yet contagion continues.
But what kind of containment measure is this that is destroying us?

This policy isn't working—we should already know that. It's the same policymakers who claim there's no money for culture, education, health, or public goods, yet when banks ask for funds or markets demand it, the money appears almost miraculously.
This economic policy is not a remedy; it is a deadly poison for growth, employment, the European economy, and the European Social Model.
Markets dictate and governments obey, threatening the people. And this is precisely what governments are doing to Greece at this moment, during an electoral period.
I sincerely hope that, on Sunday, the Greek people will not be intimidated and will instead give us a lesson, because this recipe, Madam President, is a recipe for disaster. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, dear colleagues. Unfortunately, the proposals we have discussed here today confirm the prohibition of crisis-response policies in the euro area and further entrench the logic of the fiscal treaty.

There are some statements of intent, but they are very timid. I am referring, for example, to the draft outlining a possible path toward considering ways to define potential legislation for the hypothetical introduction of eurobonds.
The problem is that this day will only come when all the issues in the eurozone have been resolved, and it is taking, and will take, much longer than expected.
We support certain aspects of this proposal, but at this pace, everything will be too little and too late.
Placing a country under legal protection entails maintaining interest rates and loan amounts, as well as prioritizing new loans. In other words, it requires,
Member states that honor their debt, yet have all and every instrument removed from them that might allow them to grow and find the resources to do so.
Let us be frank with one another. The only legal protection provided in these proposals and foreseen is for creators. But even that will not be effective, since it is not effective even for them.

As we have seen in Greece, Ireland, Portugal, and now Spain, this means condemning countries to recession, unemployment, and insolvency. And, Mr. President, I conclude by saying that the worsening of these situations and their spread to other economies brings us close to a point of no return.
Faced with the complete failure of austerity, the European Commission or the German Government offers us the same policy, but in overdose. It is neoliberal fanaticism that is killing the euro.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Given the extremely limited time we have, I simply wish to tell the Commission, as it has always been able to count on this
Parliament in effective work promoting the solidity of the eurozone and Europe, but this can only be achieved when there are initiatives focused on discipline, we

We have managed to establish the conditions for a balanced sharing of benefits and sacrifices among the different Member States and among citizens, and a hope—a hope—that we will emerge from the crisis we are currently experiencing.
For this hope to be realized, other initiatives are needed, and it is these that this Parliament is seeking to fill. An initiative focused on investment, growth, and control.
of sovereign debt, which has been the Achilles' heel of those, clearly reflecting the fragility of the economic systems of some of the member countries.
Mr. Commissioner, you can count on us, Council; Madam President, you can count on us, but the agenda must be a comprehensive one; it cannot be an agenda that does...
of the discipline the condition for everything else to follow, even making discipline the alpha and omega of our European proposals.

Our citizens expect this of us; they can count on the Parliament, and I would like to thank once again my colleagues, the rapporteurs, Mr. Gómez, the other rapporteur, and all the other colleagues who, whether as shadow rapporteurs or merely active contributors to the development of this project, have been able to make a difference

towards a more balanced, more solid parliamentary project, one that I hope will earn the respect, the capacity for dialogue, and the spirit of cooperation and compromise that it has demonstrated so far
successful. Many thanks, Mr. President. Many thanks, Madam President. Many thanks, Commissioner.

Thank you very much, Madam President. In addition to thanking all those who contributed to this outcome, I would like to request that the final vote be postponed so that we may immediately begin negotiations at the trilogue level.
Today, we face three agendas. The growth agenda, the agenda concerning bank management, and the agenda concerning sovereign debt management.
And addressing the European semester implies incorporating these three dimensions.
Regarding the issue of growth, I believe it is important at this moment to emphasize that the Commission's recommendations cannot apply solely to
countries with deficits. They must also target countries with surpluses. And if some have to suppress their domestic demand, others must release it.

I also believe that regarding growth, it is essential that the Commission oversee the recommendations from the so-called troika. Because when a country enters recession, it cannot continue to crush public investment and public spending as if nothing had happened.
The trilogues must have their draft reviewed and adjusted according to the outcomes. And I would like to tell you, Mr. Commissioner, that today we approved here in...
Parliament emphasizes the need to launch an instrument for growth and countercyclical activity amounting to approximately 1% of the European Union's GDP over a period of 10 years.
A second fundamental dimension is what is now called the Banking Union. We requested this in Parliament two years ago, in a June 2010 report. To date, it remains unfulfilled.

A third dimension is the dimension of sovereign debt. Without it, there can be no stability in Europe. And I would also like to tell you that today we approved the TUPAC here, but within the TUPAC, beyond austerity,
some of the other dimension that has been missing, which is the immediate establishment of a redemption fund, using German terminology, so that sovereign debt exceeding 60% can be managed jointly.

This is a legislative text, and we await the Commission's strong support for this Parliament initiative in order to contain the completely threatening spiral currently undermining the European Union. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. Now that we intend to discuss a growth pact, it is essential not to forget citizenship and consumers, ultimately the human and social dimension.

which gives it substance and was the subject of one of the three reports on the Single Market Act from 2001. Why should any growth pact take into account the social sacrifices and benefits associated with it?

As important as an internal market test will be conducting a social impact assessment of all measures taken to implement the single market. A test, yes, an examination that enables us to measure the progress achieved for the benefit of citizens of the European Union.
Since 2008, Europe has already lost more than 6 million jobs.
In my country, Portugal, the economy is experiencing a backlash without precedent, and unemployment among men, women, and young people is reaching historic highs.
According to forecasts by the Bank of Portugal, the situation will worsen over the next two years.
It is within this context that we support and welcome the initiative of the new employment package, and we congratulate Commissioner Andor.
Emphasizing once again and always the fundamental premise of the need for economic growth, Member States will truly have to focus their efforts on job creation.

The means to achieve this should include hiring subsidies that generate new jobs, reduction of the tax burden on labor, support for the creation of new businesses and entrepreneurship, as well as assistance to small and medium-sized enterprises that preserve existing jobs.

It is essential that national employment policies keep in mind that the areas with the greatest potential for job creation are health, information and communication technologies, and the green economy.
As stated in the communication, it is urgent to narrow the gap between the qualifications provided by education and the competences demanded by the labor market.
On the other hand, one of the solutions to combat unemployment lies in the mobility of workers.
It is also crucial that the use of structural funds for job creation and the practical importance of the Euros portal as the premier instrument for job recruitment and job offers across Europe.
Finally, social governance must be a top priority because our citizens are eagerly awaiting concrete results. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Madam President. Commissioner, it is true that six months have already passed since this scandal involving GDP, and what is most incredible is that for ten years a private company managed to
operate in the market and have introduced silicone implants intended for use in the construction and manufacturing of mattresses, which were subsequently used in women across 71 countries.
This affected some hundreds of thousands of women, as already mentioned. It is estimated that around 500,000.

Now, we are speaking in terms of estimates because there were failures—and serious failures—in this process. We know that what happened with this company is a criminal case, a fraud, a matter for court, but we also know...

There is here a fraud that must be addressed by the courts, and the courts will deal with it. But there is a legal flaw, a legal gap, and this is what we must address. We have responsibilities in this matter.

For 10 years we were unable to identify what the criminals managed to identify. Therefore, there was a failure in terms of control, traceability, file management, record-keeping—everything failed. But these individuals from this company succeeded in implementing what we were unable to control.

This process involves not only the issue of lack of information, which is indeed very important—the absence of information on all matters related to breast implants—but also the humiliation experienced by thousands of women, and particularly the humiliation endured by those women who were forced to undergo tests to determine whether they had received the correct implants or not.

We are talking about a matter of public health, and we cannot afford to be lax in matters of public health. We already regulate so many unnecessary aspects. We even regulate the authenticity of spare parts in automobiles. Just recently, we adopted a directive on this issue.

We must regulate what is essential to people's lives. The revision of the Medical Devices Directive is good news; it would have been even better if we didn't have to do it, but now that we are doing it, let us implement it with

conditions with traceability, transparency, control, and sanctions, because unfortunately, even when sanctions are applied, they will not be applied to the women who have suffered from this issue, and states must assume full responsibility.

Thank you, Mr. President. Indeed, I would like to clearly state here that for us, the Council scheduled for the end of June is crucial, and what is essential is to seek a European solution to this crisis.
From our perspective, the problem so far has been that we have been addressing each emerging issue in isolation and individually.

The time has come for us to have a comprehensive solution for Europe. A solution that, as the President Douro clearly emphasized here, entails greater integration, enhanced cooperation, closer alignment of European policies, and a renewed appreciation of the community method.
It is essential that alongside stability there be growth, but that we not abandon our concerns regarding stability.
We therefore count on a unified European response, rather than a fragmented approach tailored to each individual state or region facing difficulties.

The increased productivity and greater production possibilities led to substantial rises in milk output, creating critical situations characterized by declining supply and low prices in the milk and dairy markets.

In many regions with high productive potential, these surpluses were sold at discounted prices on the markets of other countries, further increasing downward pressure on the prices paid to producers in those countries.

This occurred, for example, with French milk sold in France at twice the price it was sold in Galicia. And producers, facing a sharp rise in the cost of raw materials and production factors, now find their ability to withstand low prices greatly reduced.

This is a poor prognosis for many farms and productive territories across Europe, and it also appears to be a clear indication of what the future may hold without quotas and without appropriate supply regulation instruments.

There must therefore be a thorough audit of the territorial impacts on thousands of farms across the European Union resulting from the abolition of these instruments currently in place to regulate supply.
Thank you, Mr. President. I begin by congratulating the rapporteur colleague on this report.
This is a matter of utmost importance for Europe.
At a time when we are experiencing a severe economic, financial, and social crisis, safeguarding intellectual property rights by combating counterfeit products is essential for the European economy.

This protection should enhance the performance of research and promote innovation. It should ensure scientific and technological knowledge, contributing to the development of new products and services.
All of this in the sense of generating growth and creating quality jobs.

Unfortunately, in recent years we have witnessed a significant increase in the violation of intellectual property rights in Europe, resulting in annual losses—as already mentioned here—of 250 million euros for businesses and the destruction of 185,000 jobs across the European region.

But beyond these negative effects on the European economy, the violation of these rights also poses a serious threat to the health and safety of European consumers, as many counterfeit products—such as foodstuffs, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and others—
They are produced in unregulated environments.
Therefore, the customs authorities of the European Union play a crucial role in preventing counterfeit products from entering the internal market.
With the revision of this regulation, the aim is to strengthen oversight of the implementation of the relevant legislation, enhance the legitimacy of its intervention, and increase the
legal clarity by adapting the provisions of the Regulation to new realities and developments, thus contributing to better protection of the European economy and consumers.
Intellectual property rights are therefore a crucial priority for achieving the objectives of the 2020 Strategy, aiming for smart, sustainable, and inclusive growth. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. Indeed, the election of the President, a new Egyptian president in the post-Mubarak era, is good news. But we cannot delude ourselves—these presidential elections in no way conclude the transition process currently taking place in Egypt.
It has been well pointed out that dissolving Parliament just days before the presidential elections brings several risks to the table, and in this case, the power concentrated in the Superior Council

The Armed Forces is at the very least an exaggeration. They have legislative power, have reduced the President's authority, and furthermore still hold in their hands the power to determine changes to the Constitution.

Now, all this raises certain concerns and should raise concerns in us, as the Egyptians risk becoming hostages to military power and a return to the past, lacking a fundamental element: a democratically elected parliament.
It is not for us to bring about change in Egypt; it is for the Egyptians themselves.
but I believe that we Europeans will have to stand alongside all changes that aim to restore democratic legitimacy and a democratically elected Parliament. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Madam President. Dear colleagues, Madam Commissioner, I begin by congratulating my fellow rapporteur, Lara Come, on the excellent work carried out in this report, which is of vital importance for Europe's future.
In a world and society undergoing rapid transformation, Europe needs a comprehensive, integrative, effective, and up-to-date standardization system.
A system that ensures the proper functioning of the internal market, improves consumer protection and their quality of life, and also safeguards the environment, is essential.

As it is also very important to foster greater innovation and social inclusion, as well as the interaction and communication among networks and systems, particularly in the domain, as already emphasized here, of new information and communication technologies.
As Professor Monti stated in his report A New Strategy for the Single Market, European standardization is essential for the governance of the internal market, highlighting the
the need to review the European standardization process, maintaining the advantages of the current system and achieving a fair balance between the European and national dimensions.

In this sense, the report we are now discussing follows and supports these correct and appropriate objectives. Eliminate the sluggishness and fragmentation that exist, which cause companies to incur costs

higher transaction costs and higher unit costs, strengthen the participation of SMEs in standardization activities, particularly at the national level, as well as facilitate their access to these standards, also strengthen

On the other hand, the involvement of stakeholders representing societal interests, such as consumers, environmentalists, workers, and persons with disabilities, should be maintained, along with financial support for European organizations established to represent public interests.

I conclude by stating that the approval of this report will contribute to revitalizing the European project, to European innovation, and to strengthening the competitiveness of our enterprises. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner. The future common fisheries policy must give priority to its environmental, economic, and social dimensions,
in a synergistic and non-hierarchical manner, in support of sustainable development and in line with the objectives of intelligent, sustainable, and inclusive growth set out in the EU 2020 strategy.
As rapporteur for the third regime, I would like to highlight the particular characteristics of certain regions, such as the outermost regions, whose economies are heavily dependent on this sector.

In these regions, the reform of the common fisheries policy should aim to ensure the future survival and prosperity of their small-scale and artisanal fishing fleets, thereby safeguarding and securing the employment dependent upon them.

I agree with the European Commission's position regarding the need to adjust this policy and to implement specific measures that take into account the differing realities of the fishing and aquaculture sectors across various coastal regions.

I argue that this policy focuses on regionalization as a governance approach, by strengthening the involvement of various stakeholders, so that, in the future, a more appropriate response to the demands and sensitivities of each maritime basin can be achieved.
We are reaching the decisive moments in which we will shape the new fishing policy in Europe. And we cannot fail.

The need and urgency to achieve sustainable balances in our aquatic resources, as well as to revitalize our coastal fishing communities, improve their socio-economic conditions, modernize the sector, and ensure the supply of high-quality food, demand courage and bold measures as never before.
We therefore need to make choices and prioritize objectives.

I proposed protection for the vulnerability of my region by suggesting that the exploitation of its resources be based on scientific data, that our coastal communities be given priority in their exploitation, and that external access be restricted beyond 100 miles from the existing fishing banks.
I proposed safeguarding the fishing capacity of our important fishing communities, rejecting the proposals for transferable fishing quotas along the coasts.

I proposed that our communities have improved involvement in managing their fishing affairs through the establishment of a dedicated Regional Advisory Council, better suited to interperipheral regions. I hope that in this way we can achieve a better future.

Given what we know about the implementation of the third package and considering that part of this issue has already been discussed today, regarding the delay in transposing its directives, the most recent information available to me indicates that

that 18 Member States have been notified, this information is probably already outdated due to lack of transposition. The question I pose to you is how you view the possibility of, in the legislation being prepared, particularly regarding telephone networks,
trans-European, if it includes a device, an incentive that conditions eligibility for the submission of PCI projects, projects of common interest, or that is

condition compliance by Member States of their obligations arising from the third package. Or, conversely, does it consider this an extravagant mechanism and that it should be left solely to the regular competition mechanisms, Article 101?
and their equivalents, all intervention in this matter. Many thanks.
from the Commission and the tolerance, the spirit of tolerance that has existed in this matter. But the issue is whether, given that legislation is being prepared that may
introduce an additional and much stronger conditionality, do you consider this conditionality to be an abusive instrument, or do you think such conditionality would be acceptable?
I confess that I share much of the concern initially raised here regarding democratic legitimacy and democratic oversight related to this proposal.
Banking Union and Banking Supervision, because a fundamental issue concerning the role of Parliament—the only institution democratically elected directly by citizens—remains unclear and unresolved.

But there is another concern I also have, one that is fundamental and relates to the following. Monetary policy instruments are controlled by the European Union. Monetary policy falls under the responsibility of the European Union.

Bank supervision has also become the responsibility of the European Union. Yet, if things go wrong and banks fail, the responsibility for covering such failures remains with the taxpayers of the national states. It remains a national responsibility.

This is not a matter of seed importance, but a fundamental issue that remains unresolved. And lastly, I would simply like to point out that the fact that we continue to tie monetary policy to what is, in our current context, a secondary concern—namely inflation—rather than to job creation and growth, as it should be, also remains unresolved. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. European leaders continue to talk about budgetary discipline, so let's talk about it. What exactly do we mean when we speak of budgetary discipline?

In my understanding, fiscal discipline means honoring contracts with pensioners and not abandoning state commitments midway. In my understanding, fiscal discipline means respecting the agreements of those who work and not inventing extraordinary taxes or cutting subsidies.

Fiscal discipline means honoring commitments in health, education, and culture, and not closing services or halting and canceling projects midway. Fiscal discipline means restoring banks to order, rather than taking money from taxpayers to hand over to speculators in order to save banks.
This is the fiscal discipline I would like to see implemented in the European Union, rather than continuing this celebration of markets.

If an example is needed, the budget currently being discussed in Portugal, at the behest of the Troika, is a perfect example. It violates every commitment any state should have toward its citizens in order not to breach the agreements made behind closed doors that are bringing Europe and its nations together.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission. First of all, I would naturally like to congratulate you on the outcomes of the Council. Although it seems somewhat delayed in timing, the final decision comes a little late.
The truth is that there is progress.
Now, precisely because I identify with everything my colleague Cássio Lídez has said, I would like to ask just one question of the President of the Council. Countries that are under assistance...
Countries facing difficulties have struggled significantly to ensure that banking debts can be applied retroactively in their separation from state debt.
This apparently was not accepted.
But yesterday we heard statements from the highest German authorities and the highest French authorities indicating that they would treat Ireland in a special manner.

What I am asking is whether the Council has approved any decision on treatment that discriminates against Ireland, differing from the treatment reserved for countries such as Portugal, Greece, Spain, and others.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Commissioner, it is a privilege to present the report on the 20 concerns of citizens and businesses
Regarding the functioning of the single market, precisely in this debate with Commissioner Barnier, marking the twentieth anniversary of this market.
A first word of thanks to colleagues from all political groups, and in particular to the rapporteurs and shadow rapporteurs, for your highly constructive contributions and your positive attitude.
It was a pleasure working with everyone, and the report greatly benefited from our combined efforts.

We are well aware and take pride in the fact that, over these 20 years, the single market has been the driving force behind major economic and employment opportunities across Europe, transforming the lives of European citizens and European businesses.
Traveling, residing, and working within the area of the Union are freedoms accessible to all citizens of the 27 member states.
For European consumers, the single market means greater choice and lower prices.
For our companies, the single market means greater business opportunities, with access to 500 million consumers, which translates into more economic growth and more job creation.
To illustrate the progress the single market has achieved in Europe since its creation in 1992 up to the onset of the crisis in 2009,
2008, one need only look at the 2.7 million jobs created and the additional 2.13% GDP growth.
Today, making or receiving a phone call while traveling in Europe is 13% cheaper than it was in 2005.
The freedom of movement of people finds its most eloquent expression in the Erasmus programme.
Over two and a half million students have benefited from this European Programme for Education and Mobility Exchange.
Over the past five years, companies have also benefited from a 25% reduction in administrative positions due to the legislation of the single market.
But one question arises. Is everything going well in the single market?
No, not everything works well.
The economic and financial crisis affecting us has had negative repercussions on the operation of this market.
Our citizens, in some countries living with the hardship of unemployment and facing austerity measures, have come to view Europe with skepticism and hold a negative perception of the internal market.
People still do not know or fully understand their rights, nor do they know where to find information or assistance.
On the other hand, many Member States unjustifiably delay the transposition of directives and European legislation on the single market, thereby undermining their full potential.

In certain areas of citizens' daily lives, things as simple as, for example, the reimbursement of medical expenses following a temporary stay in another Member State, or a student participating in an educational programme opening a bank account,

Erasmus, switching energy suppliers, transferring pension benefits from retirement, recognition of qualifications, and registering a vehicle in another Member State remain very complex, difficult, or costly actions.
It is important to address these problems.
That was my concern while serving as rapporteur.
Therefore, the message conveyed in the report is very clear. More political will is needed, much more political will from the Member States and European institutions.

Council, above all Council and Commission, to govern better and jointly. It is necessary to regain the trust of citizens and businesses, and it is essential to facilitate the exercise of their rights.

Finally, I would like to greet the European Commission, represented by Commissioner Barnier, for presenting the Act for the Single Market 2, with its 12 priority actions for renewed growth, which I am certain marks a new stage in our future. Thank you very much.
Madam President, Commissioner Barnier, and dear colleague Regina Bastos, my congratulations on your already highly successful efforts.
When Jacques Delors chaired the European Commission, the Single Market of the Union was established in 1992.
Delors then summarized the three fundamental principles of the single market: competition that stimulates, cooperation that strengthens, and solidarity that unites.
Twenty years later, the economic and financial crisis reignited the threat of national protectionism, making it clear that the internal market is not a completed project.

And the celebration of these 20 years must be seized as an opportunity for a renewal of the single market, with the aim of placing the market at the service of the citizen, rather than the citizen at the service of the market.
To this end, priorities must be established. And I would like to discuss one priority in particular.
It is essential to have a common policy to produce and distribute energy at affordable prices for consumers, with a high level of energy efficiency
through the European energy market, served by cross-border infrastructure with the potential for community leverage of investment, thereby representing added European value.
In energy as well, Madam President, the common market will be judged.
One of the greatest achievements of the European Union, undoubtedly one of the strongest pillars supporting the development of the Union, its member states, and its regions.
However, for it to be effective and fair, its policies must emphasize equal opportunities for citizens and all regions, promoting the Union's internal cohesion.

In this context, it is essential to recognize that we can only achieve greater development in our most remote regions, such as the outermost regions, through their enhanced integration into the single market.

Regions such as the Azores need to continue enhancing their competitiveness by improving their competitive conditions. But we will never succeed in doing so unless we better address our accessibility challenges in the transportation sector.
We can achieve this through specific instruments that better address the problems raised by our detachment.
We therefore rely on the Commission to consider a specific programme, akin to a post-transportation scheme, as an essential instrument for integrating these types of regions into the internal market.
Thank you very much, Madam President. I begin by thanking you for this excellent debate.
It was a very constructive debate, and one that demonstrated that the single market is indeed a major achievement of the European project.

I welcome the presence of Minister Mavroianis and his participation in this debate. I thank all my colleagues for their constructive approach, and to some, their cautious stance, which is understandable.

and to say a very heartfelt thank you to Commissioner Barnier for the dedication and enthusiasm he brings to the discussion of these issues, these challenges facing the internal market, and the political will to address them.
We still have a great deal to answer to citizens.
Citizens are dissatisfied, disillusioned, and expect more from us, expect more from the Council, expect more from the Commission, expect more from the Member States.
This review is very useful; this review of the past 20 years is important, but as many have said here, it is crucial to open the door to a look toward the future.
And the vision for the future encompasses many issues raised here and numerous measures already announced by the European Commission.
The digital single market, citizens' mobility, the reform of the Euréka portal, the European energy market are examples
eloquent testimony that we still have an important journey ahead in the future, and that this journey is of great importance and vital in addressing this time of crisis we are experiencing.
Citizens are suffering; citizens expect much from politicians and from the responses that politicians can provide to the adverse conditions they are enduring.
Therefore, it is important that the single market be one of the responses to the major challenge of competitiveness, employment, and economic growth.
I am certain that all of us, following this celebration of our 20th anniversary, are more eager than ever to provide the answers our citizens need. Thank you very much.

The Gozés report, together with the report we will prepare on the annual growth prospects for the Union at the end of this year and which will precede the Spring Council, are two essential components of the so-called European Semester.
This report addresses the recommendations made to each of the member countries by the Council on the Commission's proposal. And these recommendations are framed, as just mentioned, within the...
the SIXPEC and the future TUPEC, an extraordinarily powerful body that strengthens European control over the economic and budgetary policies of member countries.

The balance between this control and national autonomy and legitimacy is difficult to achieve and has declined to a level of imposition, particularly in terms of discipline, and in the final analysis may even trigger a serious package of sanctions.

Regarding the Tupac, I would like briefly to point out that it is important for the Council, which is currently absent here, to grant its negotiators a negotiating mandate consistent with the ambition of swiftly concluding this package, this Tupac.

But more European coordination, returning to this theme, means less control by individual countries over their own destiny, requiring a subtle balance, as well as a higher demand for the quality of recommendations made to countries across at least three or four dimensions.

First, it is essential to ensure coherence between medium- and long-term objectives, which include growth, employment, and real convergence, and short-term recommendations focused primarily on discipline and nominal convergence. Second, there must be coherence

with the conclusions that must be drawn from the analysis of macroeconomic imbalances, in particular regarding the asymmetric impact of common policies and negative externalities, as well as the spillover effects of certain national policies on other countries.

Thirdly, we must agree that there is a minimum threshold of social, economic, and labor rights that must not be exceeded by these recommendations. And lastly, it is important that the quality of the recommendations be assessed based on the results achieved—or, to date, this has been far from the case.

I conclude just now, Mr. President. I would simply like to thank Mr. Gozés for his spirit of collaboration and to hope that he takes yet another step in this spirit, so as to broaden the consensus around his report, and that this spirit will also remain in relation to the next matter.
It is true, and has already been mentioned here many times, that there are many things we do not know about shale oil extraction.
but there are also many that we already know and that we ought not to forget.
For example, we know that each shale gas extraction operation uses more than 19 million liters of potable water, enough to supply the annual needs of one thousand European citizens.

We also know that each shale gas extraction, in each operation, injects approximately 300 tons of fluids into the ground, which may include up to 300 tons of chemicals, about 80% of which remain in the soil and never leave it.

We also know that each shale gas extraction releases radioactive elements and heavy metals into the subsurface, which, together with the evaporation of toxic residues resulting from the process, constitute a real threat to public health.
This poses a risk of contamination of potable water resources, and it is a real and imminent contamination.

Knowing this, we understand that the extraction of shale gas is also a response that appears easy, focusing solely on the immediate, on industry and profit, while ignoring populations, the future, and the environment.

But this is also the Europe that knows, within this House, that water is an essential, vital, and important resource, that understands the need to protect citizens' health and must safeguard it, that seeks agreements to prevent emissions and to promote energy efficiency, because we know that environmental standards are important, as are health standards.
Therefore, when one does not know everything, to be prudent is the least one can do.
And they may even wish to do so, but let them not do it in our name or in the name of the citizens we represent here.

Thank you, Mr. President, Mr. President of the Board of Directors, Commissioner. I will be very brief, as I believe the issue at hand is essentially political, not
a financial issue. And the question that arises is the following: whether we want the European Union to make a difference in the context of the crisis, or whether we want to worsen the crisis.

Most European countries are today making a tremendous effort, from the standpoint of their public finances, to overcome the crisis in a competitive manner, in a way that can position their European countries

In the European Union, on a global scale, there is a high degree of competitiveness and competitive capacity; however, it is obvious that without investment, there can be no growth and this crisis cannot be overcome.
If the European Union insists on these drastic cuts, particularly in the cohesion policy and the common agricultural policy, what this will mean is that the countries which are making an enormous
effort, they will not succeed in growing, and this plan we have accepted in all countries for the European Union will fail. Sound accounts with prospects for growth and competitiveness.

The only major instrument for this is precisely this multiannual financial framework. And therefore, if we want the European Union to succeed and for its policy to serve as an exemplary model for other countries, we must commit to the budget for the financial perspectives.
